title: SQL Upload Guide
text:
If you do statistics or data science, your default instinct when handed a pile of CSVs is probably to load each one into R or Python and start joining data frames in memory. That, however, leads to issues including files are too big to fit comfortably in RAM, re-running the same read_csv() calls every session, or you need ten different summary tables and don’t want to re-derive each one by hand every time.
A relational database solves these problems by moving the structure of your data out of your analysis script and into the data itself. Instead of a folder of CSVs that only mean something in light of whatever script last touched them, you get a single, queryable system where the relationships between tables, the rules about what counts as valid data, and the logic for combining tables all live in one place.
Concretely, that gets you:
Speed and memory efficiency at scale. A database engine reads only the columns and rows a query actually needs, rather than loading entire files into memory first.
A single source of truth. Once data lives in a database, every person and every script that connects to it sees the same version.
Enforced data integrity. A database can refuse to let you insert nonsense: a row that references an actor who doesn’t exist, a duplicate ID where uniqueness is required. This is the part we’ll spend the most time on below: keys are the mechanism that makes this enforcement possible, and they’re worth understanding even if you never write SQL again after this guide.
Reusable, composable queries. SQL’s JOIN machinery lets you combine tables on demand, rather than writing bespoke merge logic in R every time you need a different combination of variables.
We’ll walk through all of this concretely, using a real dataset: every cast member, host, sketch, and impression from Saturday Night Live, scraped from a public repository and loaded into a MariaDB server.

title: SQL Upload Guide
section: Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database
text:
Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database

Where the Data Comes From
For this guide, the source data comes from the snldb GitHub repository, which contains a Python scraper for SNL cast, episode, sketch, and appearance data. The specifics of this scraper don’t matter much beyond this guide. What matters is the general pattern: most real-world data starts out as scraped, exported, or otherwise messy flat files, and getting it into a proper database is itself a meaningful step.
pip install -r requirements.txt
./crawl_single_episode.sh   # test run: one episode
./crawl_all.sh               # full crawl: every episode
python convert_json_to_csv.py
After running this, you end up with a folder of .csv files, one per entity type (actors, episodes, sketches, and so on). This is the starting point most statisticians actually encounter: a pile of CSVs, related to each other in ways that exist only in your head (or a README), not yet enforced anywhere.


Connecting R to a SQL Server
The general pattern for connecting to any SQL database from R is the same regardless of which database engine (MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) sits on the other end: you load a driver package, supply connection credentials, and get back a connection object that every subsequent query uses.


Show the code
library(DBI)
library(RMariaDB)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(purrr)
library(janitor)


A practice worth adopting immediately, regardless of what data you’re working with: never write credentials directly into a script. Store them as environment variables in a .Renviron file (which should never be committed to version control), and read them in with Sys.getenv().


Show the code
SQL_USER <- Sys.getenv("SQL_USER")
SQL_PWD  <- Sys.getenv("SQL_PWD")
SQL_HOST <- Sys.getenv("SQL_HOST")
SQL_DB   <- "snl"




Show the code
Sys.setenv(MySQL_TLS_DISABLE_PEER_VERIFICATION = 1)

con_snl <- DBI::dbConnect(
  RMySQL::MySQL(),
  host = SQL_HOST,
  user = SQL_USER,
  password = SQL_PWD,
  dbname = SQL_DB,
  load_data_local_infile = FALSE
)

DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)


dbListTables() is worth calling immediately after every connection, regardless of context. It’s the fastest way to confirm the connection actually succeeded and to see what already exists on the server before you touch anything.


Locating and Validating Source Files Before Upload
Before uploading anything, it’s worth building the habit of checking that your source files are what you think they are. This step generalizes far beyond SNL data: any time you’re about to bulk-load files into a database, a quick sanity pass catches problems while they’re still cheap to fix.


Show the code
csv_folder <- "~/Desktop/Summer 2026/snldb/output"

csv_files <- list.files(
  path = csv_folder,
  pattern = "\\.csv$",
  full.names = TRUE
)

csv_files
length(csv_files)

if (length(csv_files) == 0) {
  stop("No CSV files found. Check csv_folder path.")
}




Show the code
csv_check <- map_dfr(csv_files, function(file_path) {
  df <- read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  
  tibble(
    file = basename(file_path),
    rows = nrow(df),
    columns = ncol(df)
  )
})

csv_check


This produces a small summary table — file name, row count, column count — for every file about to be uploaded. It seems like a small thing, but it’s the cheapest possible defense against uploading an empty file, a truncated file, or a file you grabbed from the wrong folder.


From File Names to Table Names
SQL table names have stricter rules than file names: no spaces, no leading numbers, no arbitrary punctuation. Rather than handling this ad hoc for every file, it’s worth writing one small helper function that any future project can reuse:


Show the code
clean_table_name <- function(file_path) {
  file_path |>
    basename() |>
    str_remove("\\.csv$") |>
    janitor::make_clean_names()
}


janitor::make_clean_names() does the heavy lifting — lowercasing, replacing spaces and punctuation with underscores, and resolving duplicate names — which is exactly the kind of small, boring utility worth keeping in your personal toolkit across projects, independent of what the data actually is.


Uploading in Chunks, Not All at Once
The temptation with a clean CSV is to dump the whole thing into the database in a single INSERT statement. In practice, this is often slower and less stable than chunking the upload — especially over a network connection to a remote server, where one enormous statement either times out or simply gets rejected outright. Therefore, the safest way is to upload data in chunks.


Show the code
upload_csv_to_sql <- function(file_path) {
  table_name <- clean_table_name(file_path)
  
  message("Reading ", basename(file_path), "...")
  
  df <- readr::read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    janitor::clean_names()
  
  message("Rows in CSV: ", nrow(df))
  message("Columns in CSV: ", ncol(df))
  
  if (nrow(df) == 0) {
    stop("CSV has 0 rows: ", basename(file_path))
  }
  
  message("Uploading as table `", table_name, "`...")
  
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `", table_name, "`;"))
  
  DBI::dbCreateTable(
    conn = con_snl,
    name = table_name,
    fields = df
  )
  
  chunk_size <- 250
  n <- nrow(df)
  starts <- seq(1, n, by = chunk_size)
  
  for (start in starts) {
    end <- min(start + chunk_size - 1, n)
    chunk <- df[start:end, ]
    
    insert_sql <- DBI::sqlAppendTable(
      con_snl,
      table = DBI::Id(table = table_name),
      values = chunk,
      row.names = FALSE
    )
    
    DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, insert_sql)
    
    message("Uploaded rows ", start, " to ", end, " of ", n)
  }
  
  row_count <- DBI::dbGetQuery(
    con_snl,
    paste0("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM `", table_name, "`;")
  )
  
  message("Finished uploading `", table_name, "` with ", row_count$n_rows, " rows.")
  
  tibble::tibble(
    table_name = table_name,
    uploaded_rows = row_count$n_rows
  )
}


A few choices here are worth calling out because they generalize to any upload pipeline, not just this one:
The function drops and recreates the table before uploading, rather than appending to whatever might already be there. This makes the script idempotent: you can re-run it as many times as you want, and you’ll always end up with a clean, current copy of the data, rather than accumulating duplicate rows on every re-run.
The row-count confirmation at the end closes the loop. This is important to verify that the row count of the uploaded tables aligns with what you already knew the CSV had from the earlier validation step.


Show the code
upload_results <- purrr::map_dfr(csv_files, upload_csv_to_sql)
upload_results


At this point, every CSV is now a SQL table, with column types automatically inferred.

title: SQL Upload Guide
section: Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys
text:
Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys
Loading data into tables gets you tables in a SQL server, but not yet a SQL database in the meaningful sense. What turns a set of unrelated tables into an actual relational database is the presence of keys: the formal mechanism that encodes how tables relate to each other and what counts as a valid row.
This is the part of the process most worth understanding deeply, because the concepts here — primary keys, foreign keys, referential integrity — apply identically whether you’re working with SNL sketches, clinical trial data, survey panels, or financial transactions.

Two Types of Keys in SQL
A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies every row in a single table, with no duplicates and no missing values allowed.
A foreign key is a column in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. This is what makes joins between tables meaningful rather than coincidental.
For more information of keys along with indexing in SQL click here.


Inspecting the Database Before Changing Anything
Before assigning any keys, the first move with any unfamiliar (or freshly uploaded) database is to look at what’s actually there.


Show the code
DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE actors;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE appearances;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE casts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE characters;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE episodes;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE hosts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE impressions;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE seasons;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE sketches;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE tenure;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE titles;")


DESCRIBE shows you each table’s columns, their current data types, whether they accept missing values, and any keys already defined. This is the equivalent of running str() or glimpse() on a data frame in R. This is the first thing you should do before drawing any conclusions about the data’s structure.


The Three Requirements for a Valid Primary Key
For a column to serve as a primary key, it has to satisfy three conditions, and they don’t depend on what your data is about:

No missing values. A key that’s sometimes absent can’t reliably identify every row.
Every value is unique. If a value repeats, it can’t distinguish between the rows that share it.
It represents a natural identifier for the entity. This one is more judgment-based. Choosing a column that means something as an identifier (an actor’s ID, a patient’s ID, a transaction’s ID) rather than an arbitrary column that happens to be unique by coincidence.

For the SNL data, the natural candidates were the existing ID columns scraped along with the data: actors.aid, characters.charid, episodes.epid, impressions.impid, seasons.sid, sketches.skid, and titles.tid. This is a common situation where the data often arrives with implicit identifiers already in place.


Checking Candidates Before Committing
Rather than trust that an ID column satisfies the three conditions above, it’s worth checking directly. This test of comparing total rows, non-missing values, and distinct values is a general-purpose validity check you can reuse for any candidate key, in any dataset:


Show the code
check_key <- function(table, col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", table, "' AS table_name,
      '", col, "' AS column_name,
      COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
      COUNT(`", col, "`) AS non_missing,
      COUNT(DISTINCT `", col, "`) AS distinct_values,
      SUM(`", col, "` IS NULL) AS missing_values
    FROM `", table, "`;
  "))
}

primary_key_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_key("actors", "aid"),
  check_key("characters", "charid"),
  check_key("episodes", "epid"),
  check_key("impressions", "impid"),
  check_key("seasons", "sid"),
  check_key("sketches", "skid"),
  check_key("titles", "tid")
)

primary_key_checks


If total_rows, non_missing, and distinct_values are all equal, and missing_values is zero, the column passes the test. If any of those don’t line up, that’s valuable information before you commit to a key and this column likely should not become a primary key.


Identifying Foreign Key Relationships
Once primary keys are settled, the next question is: which columns in other tables refer back to those primary keys? This is fundamentally a modeling question about your data’s structure, and answering it well requires understanding what each table actually represents. Often times you can identify columns in tables that have the same name.
For the SNL database, this looked like: appearances links to actors, titles, characters, impressions, episodes, and seasons (an appearance is the intersection of all of these: a specific actor performing in a specific sketch, in a specific episode); casts links actors to seasons; episodes belongs to a season; hosts links hosts to episodes; titles links to episodes, seasons, and sketches; and tenure links back to actors.


Checking for Orphans Before Enforcing Anything
A foreign key constraint, once created, requires that every value in the child column exists in the parent table. But real-world data is messy, and it’s common to have values that don’t satisfy this, called orphan values. SQL won’t let you create the constraint until they’re resolved, so it’s necessary to find them first before trying to make a foreign key that won’t be accepted.


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT c.*
  FROM casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT h.*
  FROM hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")


This LEFT JOIN ... WHERE parent.key IS NULL pattern directly answers “which rows in my child table don’t have a matching parent?” regardless of what the entities actually are.
In the SNL data, this surfaced two genuine problems: some casts.first_epid values didn’t match any real episode, and one hosts.aid value didn’t match any real actor. Scraped and merged data routinely has small inconsistencies like this, and finding them is the entire point of this check.


Deciding How to Handle Orphans
Once you’ve found orphan values, you have to decide what to do about them. The options generally are: correct the value if you can determine what it should have been, delete the row if it’s clearly invalid, or set the offending value to NULL if you can’t confidently do either of the first two.


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  SET c.first_epid = NULL
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  SET h.aid = NULL
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")


Setting the value to NULL here is a deliberately conservative choice: rather than guessing at what the “correct” episode or actor should have been (and risking a false relationship that looks valid but isn’t), the missing-link information is preserved as openly missing.


Verifying the Fix
The same orphan-check pattern from before doubles as a verification step after the fix.


Show the code
check_fk <- function(child_table, child_col, parent_table, parent_col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", child_table, ".", child_col, " -> ", parent_table, ".", parent_col, "' AS relationship,
      COUNT(*) AS orphan_count
    FROM `", child_table, "` c
    LEFT JOIN `", parent_table, "` p
      ON c.`", child_col, "` = p.`", parent_col, "`
    WHERE c.`", child_col, "` IS NOT NULL
      AND p.`", parent_col, "` IS NULL;
  "))
}

fk_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_fk("appearances", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "tid", "titles", "tid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "charid", "characters", "charid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "impid", "impressions", "impid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("casts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("casts", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  check_fk("casts", "first_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("casts", "last_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  
  check_fk("episodes", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("hosts", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("hosts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  
  check_fk("titles", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("titles", "skid", "sketches", "skid"),
  check_fk("titles", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("tenure", "aid", "actors", "aid")
)

fk_checks


The test is applied across every candidate relationship, now we can see if each foreign key relationship is valid.


A Required Technical Prerequisite: Storage Engine and Data Types
Before constraints can actually be created, two technical prerequisites in MySQL/MariaDB specifically need attention.
Storage engine. Foreign keys require a storage engine that supports them: InnoDB, in MySQL/MariaDB’s case. Tables created through bulk CSV import sometimes default to a different engine, so this needs to be checked and corrected:


Show the code
tables <- DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)

for (table in tables) {
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("ALTER TABLE `", table, "` ENGINE = InnoDB;"))
}


Matching data types. A foreign key constraint requires the child column and the parent column to have compatible types. Since automatic type inference during upload is often too loose these need to be tightened up first and have their variable type altered.


Show the code
# Primary key columns
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters MODIFY charid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY epid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions MODIFY impid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons MODIFY sid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches MODIFY skid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY tid INT NOT NULL;")




Show the code
# appearances
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY tid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY charid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY impid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY sid INT;")

# casts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY sid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY first_epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY last_epid INT;")

# episodes
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY sid INT;")

# hosts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")

# titles
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY skid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY sid INT;")

# tenure
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")


Notice that actors.aid is a VARCHAR, not an integer — in the original scraped data, actor IDs are text-based codes rather than plain numbers. Every column referencing it (appearances.aid, casts.aid, hosts.aid, tenure.aid) has to match that type exactly. This is a generalizable rule: a foreign key’s type is dictated by whatever the primary key it references actually is.


Creating the Primary Keys
With types corrected and orphans resolved, creating the primary keys themselves is mechanically simple:


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters ADD PRIMARY KEY (charid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes ADD PRIMARY KEY (epid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions ADD PRIMARY KEY (impid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons ADD PRIMARY KEY (sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches ADD PRIMARY KEY (skid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles ADD PRIMARY KEY (tid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid, sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")


Two of these deserve a second look, because they illustrate situations that come up constantly in real data modeling.
Composite keys. casts uses (aid, sid) — actor ID and season ID together — as its primary key, rather than any single column. This makes sense once you think about what a row in casts represents: one actor’s membership in one season. Neither aid nor sid alone is unique (an actor appears across many seasons; a season has many actors), but the combination is.
No natural unique identifier at all. Some tables don’t have any column or combination of existing columns that’s guaranteed unique. appearances, in this case, since a single actor could in principle appear multiple times in the same sketch in the same episode, with no existing column distinguishing those occurrences. The solution is a surrogate key: a new column, with no real-world meaning, that exists purely to give every row a unique handle.


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD COLUMN appearance_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")


AUTO_INCREMENT means the database assigns each new row the next sequential integer automatically. Surrogate keys like this are extremely common in practice; in fact, in many production database designs, every table gets an auto-incrementing surrogate key by default, specifically to sidestep this exact problem before it ever comes up.


When the “Obvious” Key Doesn’t Quite Work
The original plan for hosts was a composite key of (epid, aid): the same logic as casts. But a primary key, by definition, cannot contain missing values in any of its component columns. So before committing to that plan, it’s worth checking:


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT
    SUM(epid IS NULL) AS missing_epid,
    SUM(aid IS NULL) AS missing_aid
  FROM hosts;
")




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT *
  FROM hosts
  WHERE aid IS NULL;
")


This check found exactly one row with a missing aid, likely a host who isn’t (or wasn’t, at the time of scraping) a cast member with an actor ID on file. Because of that single missing value, (epid, aid) is disqualified as a primary key outright; SQL would refuse to create it. The fix is the same surrogate-key pattern as before:


Show the code
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD COLUMN host_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")


A key design that looks correct in theory can be invalidated by a single bad value in practice, and it’s much better to discover that during a deliberate check than to discover it as a cryptic constraint-creation error later. Always verify potential keys before you commit.


Creating the Foreign Keys
With primary keys, type compatibility, and orphan values all resolved, the foreign key constraints themselves can finally be added:


Show the code
# appearances foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_title
  FOREIGN KEY (tid) REFERENCES titles(tid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_character
  FOREIGN KEY (charid) REFERENCES characters(charid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_impression
  FOREIGN KEY (impid) REFERENCES impressions(impid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# casts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_first_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (first_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_last_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (last_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

# episodes foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE episodes
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_episodes_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# hosts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

# titles foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_sketch
  FOREIGN KEY (skid) REFERENCES sketches(skid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# tenure foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE tenure
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_tenure_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")


This is the moment the database actually becomes “relational” in the formal sense. From this point forward, the database itself,enforces these relationships.


Confirming the Final Structure
Finally, it’s worth confirming what was actually built, both as a sanity check and as documentation you (or a collaborator) can return to later.


Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    column_name,
    constraint_name,
    referenced_table_name,
    referenced_column_name
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
  ORDER BY table_name, constraint_name, ordinal_position;
")




Show the code
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    GROUP_CONCAT(column_name ORDER BY ordinal_position SEPARATOR ', ') AS primary_key
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
    AND constraint_name = 'PRIMARY'
  GROUP BY table_name
  ORDER BY table_name;
")


Querying information_schema on every standard SQL database shows its own structure through this same metadata schema, so this exact query pattern (perhaps with a different table_schema name) works for inspecting keys in any database you encounter, not just this one.

title: SQL Joins
text: Joins are key operations in relational database querying that let practitioners extract information from multiple tables simultaneously.

title: SQL Joins
text: Joins are key operations in relational database querying that let practitioners extract information from multiple tables simultaneously.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Join Keys
Joins are most commonly executed using a key from both involved tables. These keys are often identical in their construction so that they can correctly identify matches between tables. To learn more about keys and their structure, see the indexes and b-trees page.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Joins: Logical vs. Physical
When executing a SQL query, joins operate on two distinct levels: the logical join (what you define in SQL syntax) and the physical join (how the query optimizer actually executes it behind the scenes). Understanding both layers is important — it can help you write more efficient queries, reduce computation costs, and diagnose performance bottlenecks. Logical Join Types Given two tables, A (m rows) and B (n rows): Inner Join - returns only the rows where table A’s join key value matches that of table B. Analogous to \(A \cap B\). Full Outer Join - returns rows both with matching and unmatched join key values from both tables, denoting missing matches as null values. Analogous to \(A \cup B\). Returns exactly \(m + n\) rows. Left/Right Outer Join - like a full outer join, but returns rows with unmatched values from only one of the involved tables, meaning it will return . Analogous to \(A \cup (A \cap B)\) or \(B \cup (B \cap A)\). Returns at most \(m + n\) rows (if every row in A matches a row in B, or vice versa). Cross Join - takes the full Cartesian product of both tables, i.e. returns every possible combination of the rows in the involved tables. Analogous to \(A \times B\). Returns \(m \times n\) rows.
# Physical Joins
Independently of which logical join you write, the query optimizer decides how to execute it under the hood. When queries run, they go through a process of creating logical query plans that eventually need to be physically implemented, and several physical join types may be selected during this implementation depending on your query engine. PostgreSQL allows for the most granular control over physical join execution settings. The three main physical join strategies are nested loops, merge, and hash. Understanding which join the optimizer uses is essential to improving the query’s efficiency.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Nested Loop Join
The nested loop join is the simplest physical strategy to understand. For simplicity, we will focus on two-table inner joins.
First, assume neither table is sorted. First, the smaller table is designated as the “outer” table and the larger table as “inner”. Staying at a fixed row in the outer table, the engine performs a sequential scan on the inner table until it finds a match, records that match, moves onto the next row of the outer table, scans the entire inner table for matches, so on and so forth.
This is why nested loop joins can cause catastrophically long runtimes with unsorted input tables – if table A has m rows and table B has n rows, the worst-case time complexity is O(m*n).
However, you can improve performance by indexing or sorting the inner table so that the engine no longer has to scan the entire inner table on each pass (the search can end early once all instances of the join key are found). Additionally, if you’re joining on primary keys, the search will end once one matching instance is found since primary keys must be unique by definition.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Merge (Sort-Merge) Join
A merge join is one of the most efficient joins — assuming the two datasets you want to join are pre-sorted. However, this requirement can cause performance issues since sorting the data first can drastically impact overall computation.
Additionally, merge joins require at least one equijoin predicate, that is, equality between key values must be at least one condition of the join. The sort-merge process itself breaks into two phases:

Sort
The dataset is split into runs that fit in memory, each run is sorted individually, and the sorted blocks are written back to disk. Runs can be sorted by indexes or explicit sorting algorithms (indexes are often faster).


Merge
The sorted runs are combined into a final result. The engine maintains a pointer on each sorted dataset and traverses both in the same direction simultaneously. As matches are found, they are stored. Once there isn’t a match, the pointer at the smallest value advances. This continues until all rows have been processed. A key advantage over nested loops is its cost profile: unlike the nested loop join where the total cost may be proportional to the product of the number of rows in the input tables, with a merge join each table is read at most once and the total cost is proportional to the sum of the number of rows in the inputs.
It’s worth noting that performance may vary when dealing with one-to-many or many-to-many relationships, and there is additional nuance in the approach for many-to-many situations.

title: SQL Joins
text:
Hash Join
To understand the hash join, it’s first important to know what a hash table is. Essentially, a hash table is a data structure that stores information in key-value pairs for more efficient lookups. It is an array of a predetermined size, where a small, representative piece of information (key) “hashes” (corresponds) to a specific index in the array.
To find specific values within the data, then, you only need to hash the key to extract the relevant information. A hash join relies on hashing and operates in two distinct phases: the build phase and the probe phase.

Build Phase
The typically smaller of the two tables (the “build” input) is scanned, and a hash table is constructed using the values of the join key. Each entry stores a tuple from the build input along with its hash value, distributing data evenly across different “buckets.”


Probe Phase
The larger table (the “probe” input) is scanned next. The join key is hashed using the same function as in the build phase for each tuple. The hash value is then used to check the corresponding bucket in the hash table for matches. If a match is found, the tuples from both inputs are combined. Hash Collisions are a potential edge case to be aware of. A hash collision occurs when two or more distinct join key values hash to the same bucket. When a collision happens, the system must check each value in the bucket to determine if a true match exists. Depending on the number of collisions and bucket size, this can slow down the join operation — however, collisions are typically kept to a minimum with a well-distributed hash function. Hash joins are commonly used for larger joins on unsorted data, as the algorithm’s time complexity is linear at O(N + M) and it does not require pre-sorted data like the merge join does.

title: SQL Joins
section: When Does the Optimizer Choose Each Strategy?
text:
When Does the Optimizer Choose Each Strategy?
In theory, both hash and merge joins perform well with large tables. A query engine will typically use a hash join when dealing with large, unsorted datasets and equality joins, especially if one table fits in memory. In contrast, a merge join is often used when both tables are pre-sorted on the join key or when performing range-based joins, as it minimizes memory usage and avoids the extra I/O work of creating a hash table. Nested loops, while the least scalable, may outperform both on small datasets or when a good index is available on the inner table, since they require the least overhead.

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
The word “pivot” covers two different operations:

Pure reshaping — changing the shape of data (long → wide) without performing any arithmetic on values. Data is redistributed into new columns; nothing is summarized or collapsed.
Cross-tabulation — reshaping plus aggregation. Multiple source rows sharing the same (row-group, column-group) combination are collapsed into a single cell via a summary function (mean, count, sum, etc.).

We’ll demonstrate both using the gapminder dataset, which records country-level statistics at five-year intervals from 1952 to 2007. The variables we’ll use are:

country — the unit of observation
continent — the geographic grouping
year — the time point (1952, 1957, …, 2007)
lifeExp — life expectancy at birth (years)



Show the code
library(gapminder)
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(duckdb)
library(RPostgres)

data(gapminder)
glimpse(gapminder)


Rows: 1,704
Columns: 6
$ country   <fct> "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", "Afghanistan", …
$ continent <fct> Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, Asia, …
$ year      <int> 1952, 1957, 1962, 1967, 1972, 1977, 1982, 1987, 1992, 1997, …
$ lifeExp   <dbl> 28.801, 30.332, 31.997, 34.020, 36.088, 38.438, 39.854, 40.8…
$ pop       <int> 8425333, 9240934, 10267083, 11537966, 13079460, 14880372, 12…
$ gdpPercap <dbl> 779.4453, 820.8530, 853.1007, 836.1971, 739.9811, 786.1134, …

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
Part 1: Pure Reshaping

The data structure
For pure reshaping, the requirement is that each combination of row-identifier and spreading-column value appears exactly once in the source data. Thus there is nothing to aggregate, only redistribute.
In our first example:

Rows: country
Columns: year (each distinct year becomes a column header)
Cells: life expectancy

Each country has exactly one life expectancy per year, so no aggregation is needed or possible. This is the defining condition for pure reshaping.


Show the code
# Confirm each country-year combination is unique
gapminder |>
  count(country, year) |>
  filter(n > 1)


# A tibble: 0 × 3
# ℹ 3 variables: country <fct>, year <int>, n <int>


No rows — every country-year combination is unique. Pure reshaping is appropriate here.


Approach 1: pivot_wider() in R (tidyverse)
pivot_wider() is the canonical R function for pure reshaping. It takes three arguments that map directly to the three pivot roles:

id_cols — the row-grouping specification (country)
names_from — the spreading dimension (year)
values_from — the measure (no aggregation, just redistribution of lifeExp)



Show the code
wide_tidyverse <- gapminder |>
  select(country, year, lifeExp) |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols     = country,
    names_from  = year,
    values_from = lifeExp
  )

wide_tidyverse


# A tibble: 142 × 13
   country `1952` `1957` `1962` `1967` `1972` `1977` `1982` `1987` `1992` `1997`

Notice the column names (1952, 1957, …, 2007) came directly from the distinct values in the year column which is the defining feature of any pivot.


Approach 2: DuckDB PIVOT (without USING)
DuckDB’s PIVOT command without a USING clause performs pure reshaping. This is equivalent to pivot_wider(). No aggregation is specified; values are simply redistributed. The output below only shows 6 rows.


Show the code
# Set up DuckDB and load the data
con_duck <- dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb())
dbWriteTable(con_duck, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

wide_duckdb <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  PIVOT (SELECT country, year, lifeExp FROM gapminder)
  ON year
  USING first(lifeExp)
")

# Sort to match tidyverse output for comparison
wide_duckdb <- wide_duckdb |> arrange(country)

head(wide_duckdb, 6)

The output is structurally identical to the pivot_wider() result, except that this output is not rounded like in Approach 1.


Approach 3: Manual reshaping in DuckDB (CASE WHEN)
The manual approach makes every column fully explicit, with no built-in pivot mechanism involved at all.


Show the code
wide_manual <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  SELECT
    country,
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1952 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1952\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1957 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1957\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1962 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1962\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1967 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1967\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1972 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1972\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1977 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1977\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1982 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1982\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1987 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1987\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1992 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1992\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 1997 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"1997\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 2002 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"2002\",
    MAX(CASE WHEN year = 2007 THEN lifeExp END) AS \"2007\"
  FROM gapminder
  GROUP BY country
  ORDER BY country
")

head(wide_manual, 6)

MAX() is used here not to aggregate multiple values, but because GROUP BY country requires an aggregate function around non-grouped columns, and since each group contains exactly one row per year, MAX() simply returns that single value.


Verifying all three outputs match


Show the code
# Align column types and order for comparison
tidy_sorted <- wide_tidyverse |>
  arrange(country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

duck_sorted <- wide_duckdb |>
  arrange(country) |>
  rename_with(~ str_remove(.x, "^lifeExp_"), -country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

manual_sorted <- wide_manual |>
  arrange(country) |>
  mutate(across(-country, as.numeric))

all.equal(tidy_sorted, duck_sorted,   check.attributes = FALSE)


[1] TRUE


Show the code
all.equal(tidy_sorted, manual_sorted, check.attributes = FALSE)


[1] TRUE


All three approaches produce identical results — the same countries, the same years as column headers, and the same life expectancy values in every cell.


Can crosstab() do pure reshaping?
Short answer: not cleanly. crosstab() always expects exactly one (row_id, category, value) triple per row of the source query, where the value has already been aggregated. In the pure reshaping case (one value per country-year), you can technically pass crosstab() a query that produces those triples althought it is a little more complicated than the previous options:


Show the code
con_local <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname   = "postgres",
  host     = "localhost",
  port     = 5432,
  user     = "sebastianpollack",
  password = "sup"
)




Show the code
# Requires a local PostgreSQL connection with tablefunc installed
dbExecute(con_local, "CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS tablefunc;")


[1] 0


Show the code
dbWriteTable(con_local, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

wide_crosstab <- dbGetQuery(con_local, '
  SELECT * FROM crosstab(
    \'SELECT country,
            year::text,
            "lifeExp"
     FROM gapminder
     ORDER BY country, year::text\',
    \'SELECT DISTINCT year::text FROM gapminder ORDER BY year::text\'
  ) AS ct(
    country text,
    "1952" float, "1957" float, "1962" float, "1967" float,
    "1972" float, "1977" float, "1982" float, "1987" float,
    "1992" float, "1997" float, "2002" float, "2007" float
  )
')

head(wide_crosstab,6)

    1997   2002   2007

This will produce the same output — but notice that crosstab() is doing exactly what pivot_wider() and the manual CASE WHEN do: retrieving one existing value per cell. crosstab()’s mandatory output-column declaration makes it more verbose and more fragile than pivot_wider() for a pure reshaping task.

title: Pivot Types: Reshaping vs Cross-Tabulation
text:
Part 2: Cross-Tabulation

The data structure
Cross-tabulation is appropriate when multiple source rows share the same (row-group, column-group) combination, and those rows must be collapsed into a single cell via an aggregation function.
In our second example:

Rows: continent
Columns: year
Cells: average life expectancy across all countries in that continent-year combination

Each continent contains multiple countries, so each (continent, year) pair has multiple source rows — aggregation is not optional, it’s required.


Show the code
# How many countries per continent-year?
gapminder |>
  count(continent, year) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = n)


# A tibble: 5 × 13
  continent `1952` `1957` `1962` `1967` `1972` `1977` `1982` `1987` `1992`
  <fct>      <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>  <int>
1 Africa        52     52     52     52     52     52     52     52     52
2 Americas      25     25     25     25     25     25     25     25     25
3 Asia          33     33     33     33     33     33     33     33     33
4 Europe        30     30     30     30     30     30     30     30     30
5 Oceania        2      2      2      2      2      2      2      2      2
# ℹ 3 more variables: `1997` <int>, `2002` <int>, `2007` <int>




Approach 1: Manual (CASE WHEN + AVG)


Show the code
ct_manual <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  SELECT
    continent,
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1952 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1952\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1957 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1957\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1962 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1962\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1967 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1967\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1972 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1972\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1977 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1977\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1982 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1982\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1987 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1987\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1992 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1992\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 1997 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"1997\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 2002 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"2002\",
    ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN year = 2007 THEN lifeExp END), 2) AS \"2007\"
  FROM gapminder
  GROUP BY continent
  ORDER BY continent
")

ct_manual

Approach 2: DuckDB PIVOT with USING avg()


Show the code
ct_duckdb <- dbGetQuery(con_duck, "
  PIVOT (SELECT continent, year, lifeExp FROM gapminder)
  ON year
  USING ROUND(AVG(lifeExp), 2)
  GROUP BY continent

")

Approach 3: crosstab() in PostgreSQL


Show the code
dbWriteTable(con_local, "gapminder", gapminder, overwrite = TRUE)

dbGetQuery(con_local, "
  SELECT * FROM crosstab(
    'SELECT continent,
            year::text,
            ROUND(AVG(\"lifeExp\")::numeric, 2)
     FROM gapminder
     GROUP BY continent, year
     ORDER BY continent, year::text',
    'SELECT DISTINCT year::text FROM gapminder ORDER BY year::text'
  ) AS ct(
    continent text,
    \"1952\" numeric, \"1957\" numeric, \"1962\" numeric, \"1967\" numeric,
    \"1972\" numeric, \"1977\" numeric, \"1982\" numeric, \"1987\" numeric,
    \"1992\" numeric, \"1997\" numeric, \"2002\" numeric, \"2007\" numeric
  )
")


This is crosstab()’s main use case: the source query performs the GROUP BY continent, year aggregation first, producing one pre-aggregated (continent, year, avg_lifeExp) triple per row, which is the three-column structure crosstab() requires. It then reshapes those triples into the wide format.


Verifying manual and DuckDB PIVOT match

manual_sorted_ct <- ct_manual |> arrange(continent)
duck_sorted_ct   <- ct_duckdb |> arrange(continent)

all.equal(manual_sorted_ct, duck_sorted_ct, check.attributes = FALSE)

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
text:
This page reports the results of two controlled benchmarking experiments examining how DuckDB query performance changes as a function of (1) the number of Parquet files queried and (2) the total number of rows in those files. Both experiments use DuckDB’s read_parquet() function to query files directly from disk without loading them into a native DuckDB database first. For background on Parquet’s internal structure and how DuckDB reads Parquet files, see the Parquet & DuckDB reference page.
Two query types are benchmarked throughout:

COUNT(*) — counts every row; requires minimal data I/O but must open and process every file and row within each file.
SELECT c5 — reads a single column across all rows; I/O-intensive but benefits from columnar storage.

Each timing result is the mean of 100 timed runs (Test 1) or 25 timed runs (Test 2), preceded by 5 warmup runs and one additional cache-primer run that is discarded, to ensure stable measurements.

This page reports the results of two controlled benchmarking experiments examining how DuckDB query performance changes as a function of (1) the number of Parquet files queried and (2) the total number of rows in those files. For background on Parquet’s internal structure and how DuckDB reads Parquet files, see the Parquet & DuckDB reference page.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
section: Test 1: Fixed Row Count, Varying File Count
text:
Test 1: Fixed Row Count, Varying File Count

Research Question
When the total number of rows is held constant, does splitting that data across more Parquet files increase query time?


Setup


Show code
library(duckdb)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(glue)




Show code
TOTAL_ROWS   <- 1000000
SPLITS       <- c(1, 4, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 200)
WARMUP_RUNS  <- 5
TIMED_RUNS   <- 100
PARQUET_DIR  <- file.path(tempdir(), "parquet_splits")


One million rows are written to disk, split evenly across varying numbers of Parquet files (1 through 200). Every configuration contains exactly the same data(incrementing integers); only the number of files differs. Each file contains ten integer columns (c1 through c10).


Show code
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

dbExecute(con, glue::glue("
  CREATE TABLE base AS
  SELECT
    row_number() OVER () AS c1,
    row_number() OVER () AS c2,
    row_number() OVER () AS c3,
    row_number() OVER () AS c4,
    row_number() OVER () AS c5,
    row_number() OVER () AS c6,
    row_number() OVER () AS c7,
    row_number() OVER () AS c8,
    row_number() OVER () AS c9,
    row_number() OVER () AS c10
  FROM range({as.integer(TOTAL_ROWS)})
"))


[1] 1e+06




Show code
dir.create(PARQUET_DIR, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

for (n_files in SPLITS) {
  folder <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, sprintf("split_%03d", n_files))
  dir.create(folder, showWarnings = FALSE)
  rows_per_file <- TOTAL_ROWS %/% n_files

  for (i in seq_len(n_files)) {
    offset <- (i - 1) * rows_per_file
    limit  <- if (i < n_files) rows_per_file else TOTAL_ROWS - offset
    path   <- file.path(folder, sprintf("part_%04d.parquet", i))
    dbExecute(con, sprintf(
      "COPY (SELECT * FROM base LIMIT %d OFFSET %d) TO '%s' (FORMAT PARQUET)",
      limit, offset, path
    ))
  }
}




Benchmark


Show code
bench_query <- function(con, sql, warmup = WARMUP_RUNS, timed = TIMED_RUNS) {
  for (i in seq_len(warmup)) dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  times_ms <- numeric(timed)
  for (i in seq_len(timed)) {
    t0 <- proc.time()[["elapsed"]]
    dbGetQuery(con, sql)
    times_ms[i] <- (proc.time()[["elapsed"]] - t0) * 1000
  }
  mean(times_ms)
}

results <- lapply(SPLITS, function(n_files) {
  glob <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, sprintf("split_%03d", n_files), "*.parquet")
  data.frame(
    n_files   = n_files,
    count_ms  = bench_query(con, sprintf("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('%s')", glob)),
    select_ms = bench_query(con, sprintf("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('%s')", glob))
  )
})

results_df <- bind_rows(results)




Results


Show code
results_df |>
  mutate(
    `Files`          = n_files,
    `COUNT(*) (ms)`  = round(count_ms, 2),
    `SELECT c5 (ms)` = round(select_ms, 2)
  ) |>
  select(`Files`, `COUNT(*) (ms)`, `SELECT c5 (ms)`) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "r", caption = "Mean query time by file count (1,000,000 rows fixed)")



Mean query time by file count (1,000,000 rows fixed)

Show code
ggplot(results_df, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = count_ms)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#185FA5", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f ms", count_ms)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#185FA5") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, " ms"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Parquet files",
    y = "Mean time (ms)",
    title = "COUNT(*) query time by file count",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 total rows held constant"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Mean COUNT(*) time as file count increases. Total rows held constant at 1,000,000.






Show code
ggplot(results_df, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = select_ms)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0F6E56", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f ms", select_ms)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#0F6E56") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, " ms"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)),
    limits = c(0, max(results_df$select_ms) * 1.2)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Parquet files",
    y = "Mean time (ms)",
    title = "SELECT c5 query time by file count",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 total rows held constant"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Mean SELECT c5 time as file count increases. Total rows held constant at 1,000,000.






Show code
scale_factor <- max(results_df$select_ms) / max(results_df$count_ms)

results_long <- results_df |>
  mutate(select_ms_scaled = select_ms / scale_factor) |>
  pivot_longer(c(count_ms, select_ms_scaled),
               names_to = "query", values_to = "ms") |>
  mutate(query = recode(query,
    count_ms         = "COUNT(*)",
    select_ms_scaled = "SELECT c5"
  ))

ggplot(results_long, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = ms,
                          colour = query, group = query)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("COUNT(*)" = "#185FA5", "SELECT c5" = "#0F6E56")) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name     = "COUNT(*) mean time (ms)",
    labels   = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), " ms"),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      transform = ~ . * scale_factor,
      name      = "SELECT c5 mean time (ms)",
      labels    = function(x) paste0(round(x), " ms")
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x      = "Number of Parquet files",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "COUNT(*) vs SELECT c5 — file count overhead",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 rows held constant across all splits"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Both query types plotted together. Dual y-axes account for the difference in absolute times between COUNT(*) and SELECT c5.






Interpretation
COUNT(*) shows a clear and consistent increase with file count even though the total number of rows never changes. This is attributable to per-file overhead: DuckDB must open, parse the footer of, and coordinate each Parquet file individually before aggregating the result. When the data is stored in a single file this overhead is incurred once; at 200 files it is incurred 200 times. Since COUNT(*) requires minimal data I/O — DuckDB can satisfy it from footer statistics alone — this per-file cost is the dominant factor and the relationship is approximately linear.
SELECT c5 is less sensitive to file count at low file numbers, because the cost of physically reading and decompressing a full column of data is substantially higher than file-opening overhead. At higher file counts (50+), the coordination overhead becomes more visible and the two trends converge. The key distinction is that for I/O-bound queries, data volume matters more than file count; for metadata-bound queries like COUNT(*), file count matters more than data volume.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
section: Test 2: Fixed File Count, Varying Row Count
text:
Test 2: Fixed File Count, Varying Row Count

Research Question
When the number of Parquet files is held constant at 50, how does query time scale with total row count? Does splitting data across multiple files provide any advantage over a single file as row counts grow?


Setup


Show code
ROW_COUNTS  <- c(100, 10000, 100000, 500000, 1000000, 2000000)
N_FILES     <- 50
WARMUP_RUNS <- 5
TIMED_RUNS  <- 25
PARQUET_DIR <- file.path(tempdir(), "parquet_rowtest")


For each row count, the same data is written in two configurations: a single Parquet file containing all rows, and 50 files each containing an equal share of rows. Both configurations hold identical data; only the number of files differs.


Show code
dir.create(PARQUET_DIR, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

for (total_rows in ROW_COUNTS) {
  dbExecute(con, "DROP TABLE IF EXISTS base")
  dbExecute(con, glue("
    CREATE TABLE base AS
    SELECT
      row_number() OVER () AS c1,
      row_number() OVER () AS c2,
      row_number() OVER () AS c3,
      row_number() OVER () AS c4,
      row_number() OVER () AS c5,
      row_number() OVER () AS c6,
      row_number() OVER () AS c7,
      row_number() OVER () AS c8,
      row_number() OVER () AS c9,
      row_number() OVER () AS c10
    FROM range({as.integer(total_rows)})
  "))

  single_dir <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("single_", total_rows))
  dir.create(single_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
  dbExecute(con, glue(
    "COPY base TO '{file.path(single_dir, 'data.parquet')}' (FORMAT PARQUET)"
  ))

  multi_dir <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("multi_", total_rows))
  dir.create(multi_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
  rows_per_file <- max(1L, as.integer(total_rows) %/% N_FILES)

  for (i in seq_len(N_FILES)) {
    offset <- (i - 1L) * rows_per_file
    limit  <- if (i < N_FILES) rows_per_file else total_rows - offset
    if (limit <= 0) break
    path <- file.path(multi_dir, sprintf("part_%04d.parquet", i))
    dbExecute(con, glue(
      "COPY (SELECT * FROM base LIMIT {limit} OFFSET {offset}) TO '{path}' (FORMAT PARQUET)"
    ))
  }
}




Benchmark


Show code
bench_query2 <- function(con, sql, warmup = WARMUP_RUNS, timed = TIMED_RUNS) {
  for (i in seq_len(warmup)) dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  times_ms <- numeric(timed)
  for (i in seq_len(timed)) {
    t0 <- proc.time()[["elapsed"]]
    dbGetQuery(con, sql)
    times_ms[i] <- (proc.time()[["elapsed"]] - t0) * 1000
  }
  mean(times_ms)
}

results2 <- lapply(ROW_COUNTS, function(total_rows) {
  single_path <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("single_", total_rows), "data.parquet")
  multi_glob  <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("multi_",  total_rows), "*.parquet")
  data.frame(
    total_rows       = total_rows,
    single_count_ms  = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('{single_path}')")),
    multi_count_ms   = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('{multi_glob}')")),
    single_select_ms = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('{single_path}')")),
    multi_select_ms  = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('{multi_glob}')"))
  )
})

results_df2 <- bind_rows(results2)




Results


Show code
results_df2 |>
  mutate(
    `Total rows`            = format(total_rows, big.mark = ","),
    `COUNT — 1 file (ms)`   = round(single_count_ms,  2),
    `COUNT — 50 files (ms)` = round(multi_count_ms,   2),
    `SELECT — 1 file (ms)`  = round(single_select_ms, 2),
    `SELECT — 50 files (ms)`= round(multi_select_ms,  2)
  ) |>
  select(6:10) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "r", caption = "Mean query time by row count (file count fixed at 1 vs 50)")

ggplot(count_long2, aes(x = total_rows, y = mean_ms,
                         colour = condition, group = condition)) +

  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 2), " ms")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("1 file" = "#185FA5", "50 files" = "#A32D2D")) +
  labs(
    x      = "Total rows (log scale)",
    y      = "Mean time (ms)",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "COUNT(*) — 1 file vs 50 files",
    subtitle = "File count fixed; row count varies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





COUNT(*) mean time by total row count. The 50-file series consistently exceeds the single-file series due to fixed per-file overhead.






Show code
select_long2 <- results_df2 |>
  select(total_rows, single_select_ms, multi_select_ms) |>
  pivot_longer(-total_rows, names_to = "condition", values_to = "mean_ms") |>
  mutate(condition = recode(condition,
    single_select_ms = "1 file",
    multi_select_ms  = "50 files"
  ))

ggplot(select_long2, aes(x = total_rows, y = mean_ms,
                          colour = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = label_comma(), breaks = ROW_COUNTS) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 2), " ms")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("1 file" = "#185FA5", "50 files" = "#0F6E56")) +
  labs(
    x      = "Total rows (log scale)",
    y      = "Mean time (ms)",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "SELECT c5 — 1 file vs 50 files",
    subtitle = "File count fixed; row count varies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





SELECT c5 mean time by total row count. Both series grow together as data volume increases; the gap between them narrows relative to total query time.






Interpretation
COUNT(*) exhibits a stable gap between the single-file and 50-file configurations across all row counts. The absolute difference between the two series changes little as rows increase, confirming that the overhead is driven by file count rather than data volume. At low row counts this overhead represents a large proportion of total query time; at high row counts it becomes relatively smaller but remains present and consistent.
SELECT c5 both series grow together as row count increases because the cost is dominated by reading and decompressing column data, a cost that scales with the number of rows, not the number of files. At larger row counts, the relative gap between the single-file and 50-file configurations shrinks as a proportion of total query time, because the cost of reading and decompressing large volumes of column data becomes the dominant factor and diminishes the relative contribution of per-file overhead.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
text:
Conclusion
Taken together, the two experiments reveal a consistent pattern in how DuckDB handles Parquet files directly from disk. File count and row count impose costs through different mechanisms and affect query types differently.
For COUNT(*), which is the most basic form of a row-based query, file count is the primary cost driver. Because DuckDB satisfies this query from Parquet footer metadata rather than reading row data, the per-file overhead of opening and parsing each file is the bottleneck. Adding more files increases query time consistently regardless of how many rows those files contain.
For SELECT c5, which is a basic form of column-based querying, row count and data volume have a greater impact on query efficiency compared to transactional row-based query’s. The cost of reading and decompressing a column grows with the number of rows, and per-file overhead is comparatively smaller.
The practical implication is that the optimal Parquet file organization depends on the query pattern. Workloads dominated by lightweight aggregations and metadata queries benefit from fewer, larger files. Workloads involving large column scans across many rows are less sensitive to file count and may benefit from parallelism across multiple files. Neither extreme, one massive file or thousands of tiny files, is universally optimal.

  01
  
    Foundations
    What is SQL & how do tables work?
    SQL is the language of relational databases. Instead of storing everything in one spreadsheet or file, SQL stores related pieces of information in separate tables. Queries combine those tables only when needed.
