        Defining keys in SQL
        
CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(100),
  major      VARCHAR(100)
);
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
  enrollment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  student_id    INT,
  course_id    INT,
  FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
    REFERENCES students(student_id)
);
-- Add a primary key after creation:
ALTER TABLE students
ADD PRIMARY KEY (student_id);
-- Add a foreign key after creation:
ALTER TABLE enrollments
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_student
FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
REFERENCES students(student_id);
        
        
          
            PRIMARY KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE
          
          
            FOREIGN KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ADD CONSTRAINT
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · SQL Foundations
      SQL is declarative and remote
      SQL (Structured Query Language) lets you create, query, update, and manage relational databases. A relational database stores data in tables — rows are records, columns are attributes/variables.
      The key insight: SQL is declarative. You specify what data you want; the database engine's optimizer figures out how to retrieve it efficiently. This is why the same SQL query can run substantially faster with an index than without.
      SQL queries are sent to a server (SQL server) where the database is stored, though this server is often remote, that doesn't have to be the case. SQL handles everything in executing the query and returns the resulting data to the user.
      On the left is an example of a SQL query on the table students that retreives the name and major of Sophmore students in the table.
      
        Declarative
        Relational
        Structured
        Query Language
      
    

    
      1.2 · Tables & Relationships
      Relational aspect of SQL and Keys
      A table represents an entity — students, courses, orders. Each row is one record; each column is one attribute. A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
      A primary key must be unique and never NULL. Most tables have one primary key, though composite keys (multiple columns) are valid and identify rows through a composition of multiple columns.
      Relational databases become powerful when multiple tables are connected. To do this: A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key. A foreign key doesn't have to be made up of unique values but it does need to reference a primary key (which is made up of unique values). It enforces referential integrity — you cannot enroll a student who doesn't exist. The database prevents the inconsistency at the constraint level.
      The student_id column bridges the students and enrollments tables which enables powerful joins without redundancy. A foreign key defines and enforces a relationship between tables.
      
        Entities
        Attributes
        No Duplication
        Joins
      
    

    
      1.3 · Primary & Foreign Keys
      How to create keys in context
      On the left is the SQL command for creating primary and foreign keys in a SQL database.
      The CREATE TABLE command is used when one creates the key at the same time as creating the SQL table itself.
      The ALTER TABLE command is used when the table was already created but has no pre-defined key. For a primary key within the query one would use ADD PRIMARY KEY and for a foreign key within the query one would use ADD CONSTRAINT and must specify the constraint is a foreign key and the primary key that the foreign key references.
      Common confusion: a foreign key is simply a column in your table. The relationship is established from by declaring it as a reference to another table's primary key with a FOREIGN KEY constraint.
      
        Unique
        Not NULL
        Referential Integrity
        Composite Keys
      
    

  




  
    Key insight
    Keys define relationships. Indexes make those relationships fast to query.
    A primary key is a logical constraint (a column). An index is the underlying data structure the database mainatains to turn a key lookup from O(n) into O(log n). They coexist but are fundamentally different things.
  




  02
  
    Performance Structures
    Indexes
    An index is a separate ordered data structure that helps the database find rows faster by storing indexed column values and references to the corresponding table rows.
  





  

    
    
      
        Physical rows vs. Index order
        
          
            Physical table
            Row 1 Tom
            Row 2 Alex
            Row 4 Nina
            Row 7 Maria
            Row 9 Sam
          
          →
          
            Index on name
            Alex → Row 2
            Maria → Row 7
            Nina → Row 4
            Sam → Row 9
            Tom → Row 1
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_name
  ON students(name);
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_student
  ON enrollments(student_id);
        
      
    

    
    
      
        ❌ Full table scan (no index)
        
        Every row checked. Complexity: O(n).
        ✅ Index lookup
        
          Root
          →
          Branch
          →
          Leaf
          →
          Match!
        
        
          
            O(n)
            Full scan
          
          
            O(log n)
            Index lookup
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Index tradeoffs
        
          
            ✅ Reads
            Faster WHEREFaster JOINsEfficient ORDER BYFast range queries
          
          
            ⚠️ Writes
            Slower INSERTSlower UPDATESlower DELETEExtra storage
          
        
        
        Selectivity matters
        
          
            email = 'x@example.com'High ✓
            
            Matches ~1 row of millions
          
          
            status = 'active'Low ✗
            
            Matches 60% of rows — index barely helps
          
          
            country = 'US'Medium
            
            Depends on data distribution
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · What is an Index
      An index is a sorted, separate access structure
      An index is a separate data structure — like a phonebook or a textbook's back index — that helps the database find rows without reading every single one.
      The physical rows in a table can be in any order. An index stores keys in sorted logical order with references (row pointers) to where those rows actually live. The table itself doesn't move.
      Important: a key is a logical constraint (unique, not null). An index is a physical performance structure. Primary keys usually get an index automatically in MySQL/InnoDB — but the concepts are distinct.
      
        Sorted Keys
        Row References
        Separate Structure
        Faster Lookup
      
    

    
      2.2 · Why Indexes Help
      Advantage: Prevents scanning every row, by following a guided path
      Without an index on email, finding WHERE email = 'x@example.com' requires inspecting every row — a full table scan. With millions of rows this leads to computing inefficiencies and prolonged runtimes.
      With an index, the database follows an ordered tree path — root → branch → leaf — touching only a handful of nodes to land on the match. Essentially, an index cuts down the total number of comparisons needed to identify a row. This is the difference between O(n), which is the runtime without an index, and O(log n) which is the decreased runtime through an index.
      Indexes are most impactful for WHERE filters, JOIN conditions, ORDER BY sorting, and range queries like BETWEEN. Below the structure and functionality of an index is explained in depth.
      
        WHERE clauses
        JOIN conditions
        ORDER BY
        Range queries
      
    

    
      2.3 · Limitations & Tradeoffs
      Indexes not always fast and beneficial
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must also update every relevant index which causes the structure of the index to alter. Too many indexes increase maintenance cost and storage of the database.
      Selectivity is critical. An index on a column with few distinct values (like a boolean is_active or status) may not help — too many rows match, and the database may choose a full scan anyway since that may be more efficient.
       Although primary keys must be unique by definition, a regular index (created with CREATE INDEX) has no uniqueness requirement at all. Sometimes, regular indexes can be helpful to speed up row retrieval and querying even if they aren't for primary keys. It is still important to consider selectivity or uniqueness when creating a regular index, as an index on a column with low selectivity may not provide performance benefits and can even degrade performance due to the overhead of maintaining the index and the potential for increased table lookups.
        
      Even with a perfect index, a query can still be slow if it matches many rows (leaf scanning) or those rows are scattered across many storage pages and has to perform many table lookups. Table lookups happen when the database engine jumps from the index back to the main table to retrieve a full row of data after finding a match in the index. This is not a problem in MySQL because the table data lives directly in the primary key's B+ tree leaves (clustered index), so there's no second trip. Secondary indexes, however, (on non-primary-key columns) always require a second lookup back to the primary key tree to get the full row.
      
        Write overhead
        Low selectivity
        Leaf scanning
        Table lookups
      
    

  




  
    So what structure does an index use?
    Most database indexes are B trees. MySQL uses B+ trees which are a balanced tree structure designed for disk-based storage with high fanout and sorted leaf chains.
    Understanding the tree explains why indexes are fast for both equality lookups and range queries, and why they stay efficient even with millions of rows.
  




  03
  
    Data Structures
    B+ Trees
    The tree structure that makes indexes fast and optimized in MySQL.
  





  

    
    
      
        
          B+ Tree Structure
        
        
          
        
        
          Root node (top) → branch nodes (middle) → leaf nodes (bottom, linked in sorted order)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Search path for key 57
        
          
            
              
                
              
              
                
              
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            

            
            39 | 83 | 98
            
            < 39
            46|53|57|83
            83-98
            
            42|48|51
            55 | 57 | 57
            61|72|79
            
            
            KEY 57 FOUND ✓
          
        
        
          1Root [39|83|98]: 57 > 39 and < 83 → middle pointer
          2Branch [46|53|57|83]: 57 = 57 → leaf pointer
          3Leaf [55|57|57]: key found, return row reference
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Why B+ trees stay shallow
        
          Fanout of 100 → 3 levels covers
          1,000,000
          leaf entries with only 3 page reads
        
        
        
          
            ROOT
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Br
            Br
            Br
            ...
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            ...
          
        
        
          
            Equality
            Root → leaf in O(log n)
          
          
            Range
            Walk sorted leaf chain
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · B+ Tree Structure
      Root, branches, leaves
      A B+ tree has three node types. The root at the top is the entry point for every search. Branch nodes in the middle store only keys and pointers — they guide the search but hold no row data. Leaf nodes at the bottom store the actual index entries and row references.
      In MySQL/InnoDB, the primary key index is clustered — the actual table row data lives in the leaf nodes of the primary key B+ tree. Secondary indexes store the indexed column value plus the corresponding primary key, requiring a second tree lookup to retrieve the full row.
      
        Root node
        Branch nodes
        Leaf nodes
        Clustered index
      
    

    
      3.2 · Leaf Chain
      Leaf nodes are linked for range scans
      One of the key advantages of B+ trees over regular binary trees: leaf nodes are linked left-to-right in sorted order. This dashed green chain in the diagram is what enables efficient range queries.
      To answer WHERE score BETWEEN 43 AND 67, the database traverses the tree to find 43, then simply walks the leaf chain to collect everything up to 67 — no re-traversal needed.
      
        Sorted chain
        Range scans
        BETWEEN
        ORDER BY
      
    

    
      3.3 · Tree Traversal
      Tree traversal walk-through
      Consider the task where the goal is to search for a key 57: start at the root [39|83|98]. Since 57 is between 39 and 83, follow the middle pointer. At branch [46|53|57|83], find the position of 57 and follow to the leaf [55|57|57]. Found.
      Notice that only one node is visited per level. This is always true for any key lookup in a B+ tree, regardless of the key or the size of the data. At each node, the search key is compared against the stored keys and exactly one pointer is followed downward. The result is that the number of nodes inspected is always exactly equal to the depth of the tree. This is where the O(log n) runtime comes from: each level cuts the search space by the branching factor, and the tree's depth grows only logarithmically with the number of rows.
      
        O(log n)
        One path
        Exact lookup
      
    

    
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Shallow trees handle millions of rows in 3–4 reads
      Each node in a B+ tree corresponds to one page (typically 16 KB in MySQL/InnoDB). Because a page can hold hundreds of keys, the fanout — the number of entries per node — is very high.
      With fanout 100: level 1 (root) covers 100 branches. Level 2 covers 10,000. Level 3 covers 1,000,000 leaves. Therefore, a tree covering a million rows reads only 3 pages for any lookup. That's why B+ trees scale so well.
      The tree is always balanced — all leaf nodes sit at the same depth — because insertions and deletions trigger automatic node splits and merges that propagate up the tree to maintain this property. This guarantees predictable search time regardless of which key you look up.
      
        Efficient
        Always balanced
        Predictable
      
    


 
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Index structures differ across SQL dialects
      MySQL primarily uses B+ trees to structure its indexes, but other dialects treat indexing slightly differently. For example, PostgreSQL and SQLite default to B tree indexing, where (among other differences) row data is stored in all levels of the tree rather than just in the leaves. In Postgres, it is also possible to manually choose alternative indexing strategies like hash (simple and efficient for equality comparisons) or GiST operating classes (for geometric data and nearest-neighbor queries). Generally, though, understanding the structure of B and B+ trees is essential to creating and working with indexes in SQL.  
      
        B vs. B+ trees
        Manual index structure selection
      
    

  




  04
  
    Best Practices
    Practical indexing: What to take away from indexing
    Knowing the theory behind indexes can be helpful but only if you know how to utilize the information for your own good.
  





  

    
    
      
        Composite index: (customer_id, order_date)
        
          Sorted by customer_id first, then order_date within each customer:
          cust: 12024-01-05
          cust: 12024-03-12
          cust: 12024-07-01
          cust: 22024-02-18
          cust: 22024-09-30
          · · ·

          
            cust: 10
            2024-01-09 ← found
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_cust_date
  ON orders(customer_id, order_date);
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 10
ORDER BY order_date;
        
      
    

    
    
      

        
        
          SQL indexes: OLTP vs OLAP
        

        
        

          
          
            
              ✅ OLTP
            
            Transactional
            
              Finds few specific rows
              B+ tree skips most data
              Joins on foreign keys
              WHERE, lookup, update
            
            O(log n) and index beneficial
          

          
          
            
              ⚠️ OLAP
            
            Analytical
            
              Scans many/all rows
              Index can't help a full scan
              Aggregates whole columns
              AVG, SUM, GROUP BY
            
            O(n) and index not beneficial
          

        

        
        
        
          
            Use SQL + B+ tree indexes for transactional row lookups.
            Use DuckDB / Snowflake for analytical column scans.
          
        

      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Composite Indexes
      Column order (during creation) in composite indexes is critical
      A composite index on (customer_id, order_date) sorts first by customer_id, then by order_date within each customer group. This makes queries that filter by customer_id alone very efficient.
      It also helps queries filtering by both columns, and sorting by order_date after filtering by customer_id. However, a query filtering only by order_date won't benefit much — the index isn't sorted by that column alone.
      What to take away: put the column you filter on most frequently first when creating a composite index. Doing so improves both lookup speed and query efficiency compared to any other column ordering.
      
        Leading column first
        Prefix matching
        Column order matters
      
    

    
      4.2 · Guidelines
      Indexes make SQL fast for row-based, transactional queries
      SQL indexes are purpose-built for OLTP — Online Transactional Processing. These are queries that locate and return a small number of specific rows: find one customer, update one order, look up one student. The B+ tree traverses from root to leaf in O(log n) and lands on exactly the rows you need, touching almost nothing else in the table.
      This is fundamentally different from analytical queries — the kind that ask "what is the average grade across all students?" or "how many orders were placed per month this year?" Those queries need to scan many or all rows and aggregate across entire columns. An index does not help here because there is no shortcut — every row must be read regardless. This workload is called OLAP (Online Analytical Processing).
      SQL finds the right rows fast through its indexes, but analytical queries need to read one column across millions of rows meaning the database still has to touch every row's storage page. This makes analytical queries less efficient in SQL compared to transactional queries (row-based). However, based on the index model of SQL, SQL is especially efficient and strong for row-based queries.
      The practical takeaway: use SQL and its B+ tree indexes for transactional workloads like precise lookups, joins on foreign keys, filtering by specific values. For large-scale analytical work across many rows and few columns, a column-oriented system is the better architectural choice (like DuckDB or Snowflake).
      
        OLTP
        Row lookup
        OLAP
        Column scan
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      SQL is a declarative language, a query is sent to a remote SQL server, and the optimizer decides how to retrieve it efficiently. Rows are records, columns are attributes.
    
    
      02
      Primary keys uniquely identify rows and must be unique and never NULL. Foreign keys are columns that reference another table's primary key. They create relationships and enforce referential integrity. Both can be defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE.
    
    
      03
      Indexes are separate physical structures, distinct from keys, that store column values in sorted order with row references. They reduce lookups from O(n) full scans to O(log n) guided tree traversal. Composite indexes require putting the most-filtered column first for maximum efficiency.
    
    
      04
      B+ trees power MySQL indexes. The root is the entry point, branch nodes guide the search, and leaf nodes store the data. Leaf nodes are linked in sorted order enabling fast range scans. With high fanout, just 3–4 levels can cover millions of rows.
    
    
      05
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update the index structure. Low-selectivity columns (like booleans or status fields) benefit little from indexing since too many rows match.
    
    
      06
      SQL indexes excel at OLTP, precise row lookups, joins on foreign keys, transactional queries. For OLAP analytical workloads that scan entire columns across millions of rows, column-oriented systems like DuckDB or Snowflake are the better architectural choice.

title: SQL Joins
text:
● Relational Query Operations
    SQLJoins
    What is a join? How do logical join types differ from the physical strategies the optimizer actually runs — nested loops, sort-merge, and hash?
    
      Join Keys
      Logical Joins
      Physical Strategies
      Optimizer Choices
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    What are joins & join keys?
    Joins are key operations in relational database querying that let practitioners extract information from multiple tables simultaneously. Most joins are executed using a key shared by both tables — keys whose construction matches so they can correctly identify matches between tables.
  





  
  

    
    
      
        Two tables joined on a shared key
        
          
            
              departments
              KEYdept
              dname
            
            
              dept
              ↔
              links
            
            
              buildings
              KEYdept
              building
            
          
          



dept
dname
building




2
Art
STAR


5
Math
Estella




          The dept column exists in both tables. A join matches rows where the two keys are equal, combining their columns into one result.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Logical join types · A (m rows), B (n rows)
        
          
            
              
              
              
            
            Inner JoinOnly rows where A's key matches B's. A ∩ B
          
          
            
              
              
            
            Full Outer JoinMatched and unmatched rows from both, nulls for misses. A ∪ B · returns m + n
          
          
            
              
              
              
            
            Left / Right Outer JoinAll rows from one side, matches from the other. A ∪ (A ∩ B) · at most m + n
          
          
            
              
              ×
              
            
            Cross JoinEvery combination — the Cartesian product. A × B · returns m × n
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · Join Basics
      Joins combine rows across tables
      A join lets you query multiple tables at once, pulling related information into a single result set. Rather than duplicating data, relational databases keep it spread across tables and stitch it together at query time.
      Joins are most commonly executed using a key from both involved tables. These keys are often identical in their construction so that they can correctly identify matches between tables.
      To learn more about keys and their structure — primary keys, foreign keys, and the indexes that make them fast — see the indexes and B-trees page.
      
        Multiple tables
        Shared key
        No duplication
      
    

    
      1.2 · Logical Join Types
      The four logical joins you can write
      Given two tables, A (m rows) and B (n rows), the logical join type defines what the result should contain:
      An inner join returns only rows where A's join key matches B's — analogous to A ∩ B. A full outer join returns matching and unmatched rows from both tables, denoting missing matches as nulls — analogous to A ∪ B, returning exactly m + n rows.
      A left or right outer join is like a full outer join but keeps unmatched rows from only one side — analogous to A ∪ (A ∩ B), returning at most m + n rows. A cross join takes the full Cartesian product, every possible combination of rows — analogous to A × B, returning m × n rows.
      
        Inner · A ∩ B
        Full outer · A ∪ B
        Left / Right
        Cross · A × B
      
    

  




  
    Two layers, one query
    The logical join says what you want. The physical join decides how the engine gets it.
    Understanding both layers helps you write more efficient queries, reduce computation costs, and diagnose performance bottlenecks. The same logical join can run with radically different speed depending on the physical strategy the optimizer picks.
  



  02
  
    Under the Hood
    Logical vs. physical joins
    Independently of which logical join you write, the query optimizer decides how to execute it. Queries pass through logical query plans that must be physically implemented, and several physical join types may be selected depending on your engine. The three main strategies are nested loops, sort-merge, and hash.
  





  

    
    
      
        From SQL text to executed join
        
          
            SELECT * FROM A JOIN B  ON A.dept = B.dept;
          
          ↓ logical join (what)
          Optimizer builds & compares query plans
          ↓ physical join (how)
          
            NestedLoop
            Sort-Merge
            Hash
          
          PostgreSQL allows the most granular control over physical join execution settings. Knowing which join the optimizer uses is essential to improving a query's efficiency.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · Two Levels of a Join
      What you write vs. how it runs
      When executing a SQL query, joins operate on two distinct levels: the logical join (what you define in SQL syntax) and the physical join (how the query optimizer actually executes it behind the scenes).
      When queries run, they go through a process of creating logical query plans that eventually need to be physically implemented. Several physical join types may be selected during this implementation depending on your query engine.
      PostgreSQL allows for the most granular control over physical join execution settings. The three main physical join strategies are nested loops, merge, and hash — and understanding which one the optimizer uses is essential to improving the query's efficiency.
      
        Logical = what
        Physical = how
        Query plans
      
    

  




  03
  
    Physical Strategies
    The three physical joins
    Nested loops, sort-merge, and hash. Each has a distinct algorithm, cost profile, and sweet spot. The animations below walk through exactly how each one matches rows and assembles the result.
  





  

    
    
      
        Nested loop · scan inner for each outer row
        
          
        
        The outer row (green) stays fixed while the engine scans the entire inner table for matching keys, appending each match to the result.
      
    

    
    
      
        Nested loop on a unique key · search stops early
        
          
        
        When the inner key is unique (a primary key), the scan ends as soon as the single match is found — no need to scan the rest of the inner table.
      
    

    
    
      
        Why nested loops can be slow
        
          Worst case · unsorted inputs
          O(m × n)
          every outer row scans the whole inner table
        
        
          ✓Index or sort the inner table so the scan ends early once all matching keys are found.
          ✓Join on a primary key and the search ends after one match, since primary keys are unique by definition.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · Nested Loop Join
      The simplest strategy: scan for every row
      The nested loop join is the simplest physical strategy to understand. For simplicity we focus on two-table inner joins, and assume neither table is sorted.
      The smaller table is designated the outer table, the larger the inner. Staying at a fixed row in the outer table, the engine performs a sequential scan on the inner table until it finds a match, records that match, moves to the next outer row, scans the entire inner table again, and so on.
      
        Outer × inner
        Sequential scan
        Simplest to grasp
      
    

    
      3.2 · Unique-Key Shortcut
      A unique inner key ends the search early
      You can improve performance dramatically by indexing or sorting the inner table, so the engine no longer has to scan the entire inner table on each pass — the search can end early once all instances of the join key are found.
      Additionally, if you're joining on primary keys, the search ends once a single matching instance is found, since primary keys must be unique by definition. The animation shows the scan halting at the first (and only) match.
      
        Early stop
        Unique key
        Indexed inner
      
    

    
      3.3 · Cost Profile
      Catastrophic on large unsorted inputs
      This is why nested loop joins can cause catastrophically long runtimes with unsorted input tables. If table A has m rows and table B has n rows, the worst-case time complexity is O(m × n).
      That said, nested loops require the least overhead, so they may outperform the other strategies on small datasets or when a good index exists on the inner table — exactly the cases shown in the two animations above.
      
        O(m × n) worst case
        Great when small
        Low overhead
      
    

  



  
    From quadratic to linear
    Nested loops can cost m × n. Sort-merge and hash read each table about once — cost proportional to m + n.
    The next two strategies trade a little setup work — sorting, or building a hash table — for a dramatically better cost profile on large inputs.
  





  

    
    
      
        Sort-merge · phase 1: sort each input
        
          



dept
dname




2
Art


5
Math


7
Music




          



dept
building




2
STAR


5
Estella


7
Thatcher


9
Seaver




        
        Each input is split into memory-sized runs, each run sorted individually (by index or sort algorithm), then written back — both inputs sorted on the join key.
      
    

    
    
      
        Sort-merge · phase 2: merge with two pointers
        
          
        
        A pointer walks each sorted table in the same direction. On a match (teal) the row joins the result; otherwise the pointer at the smaller value advances.
      
    

    
    
      
        Cost: each table read at most once
        
          Merge cost (already sorted)
          O(m + n)
          proportional to the sum, not the product
        
        
          ⚑Requires at least one equijoin predicate — equality on the key must be a join condition.
          ⚠The sort requirement can dominate cost if the data isn't already sorted.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.4 · Merge (Sort-Merge) Join
      Most efficient — if the data is pre-sorted
      A merge join is one of the most efficient joins, assuming the two datasets are pre-sorted. That requirement is also its catch: sorting first can drastically impact overall computation.
      Merge joins require at least one equijoin predicate — equality between key values must be at least one condition of the join. In the sort phase, the dataset is split into runs that fit in memory, each run is sorted individually, and the sorted blocks are written back to disk. Runs can be sorted by indexes or explicit sorting algorithms — indexes are often faster.
      
        Equijoin required
        Sort phase
        Memory-sized runs
      
    

    
      3.5 · The Merge Phase
      Two pointers sweep the sorted runs
      The sorted runs are combined into a final result. The engine maintains a pointer on each sorted dataset and traverses both in the same direction simultaneously.
      As matches are found, they are stored. Once there isn't a match, the pointer at the smallest value advances. This continues until all rows have been processed — exactly the sweep shown in the animation.
      
        Twin pointers
        Advance smaller side
        Single pass
      
    

    
      3.6 · Cost Profile
      Cost scales with the sum, not the product
      A key advantage over nested loops is the cost profile: unlike the nested loop join, where total cost may be proportional to the product of the row counts, with a merge join each table is read at most once and total cost is proportional to the sum of the input row counts.
      It's worth noting that performance may vary with one-to-many or many-to-many relationships, and there is additional nuance in the approach for many-to-many situations.
      
        O(m + n)
        Read once each
        Many-to-many nuance
      
    

  





  

    
    
      
        A hash table: keys → buckets
        
          key
          → hash() →
          bucket #
        
        
          5  Math
          7  Music
          2  Art
        
        A hash table stores key-value pairs in a fixed-size array. A key "hashes" to a specific bucket, so lookups need only hash the key — no scanning.
      
    

    
    
      
        Hash join · build, then probe
        
          
        
        Build (orange): hash each row of the smaller table into a bucket. Probe (teal): hash each row of the larger table and check its bucket for a match.
      
    

    
    
      
        Collisions & cost
        
          Linear in the inputs
          O(N + M)
          no pre-sorting required
        
        
          Hash collision
          Two distinct keys land in the same bucket. The system checks each value in the bucket for a true match. A well-distributed hash function keeps collisions minimal.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.7 · Hash Join
      First, what is a hash table?
      To understand the hash join, it helps to know what a hash table is: a data structure that stores information in key-value pairs for efficient lookups. It's an array of predetermined size, where a small representative piece of information — the key — hashes (corresponds) to a specific index in the array.
      To find a value, you only need to hash the key to extract the relevant information. A hash join relies on this and operates in two distinct phases: the build phase and the probe phase.
      
        Key-value pairs
        Buckets
        Fast lookup
      
    

    
      3.8 · Build & Probe
      Build a table, then probe it
      In the build phase, the typically smaller table (the build input) is scanned and a hash table is constructed using the join-key values. Each entry stores a tuple along with its hash value, distributing data evenly across buckets.
      In the probe phase, the larger table (the probe input) is scanned. Each tuple's join key is hashed with the same function, and that hash points to the bucket to check for matches. If a match is found, tuples from both inputs are combined — as the animation shows.
      
        Build · smaller table
        Probe · larger table
        Same hash function
      
    

    
      3.9 · Collisions & Cost
      Linear cost, watch for collisions
      A hash collision occurs when two or more distinct join-key values hash to the same bucket. The system must then check each value in the bucket to determine a true match. Depending on collision count and bucket size, this can slow the join — but a well-distributed hash function keeps collisions minimal.
      Hash joins are commonly used for larger joins on unsorted data, as the algorithm's time complexity is linear at O(N + M) and it does not require pre-sorted data the way a merge join does.
      
        O(N + M)
        Collisions
        No sorting needed
      
    

  



  04
  
    Decisions
    When does the optimizer choose each?
    Knowing the three strategies is only useful if you know when each one wins. The optimizer weighs data size, sortedness, the join predicate, and available indexes to pick the cheapest plan.
  





  

    
    
      
        Choosing a physical join
        
          
            Nested Loop
            Small datasets, or a good index on the inner table. Least overhead.
          
          
            Sort-Merge
            Both inputs already sorted on the key, or range-based joins. Minimal memory.
          
          
            Hash
            Large, unsorted data with equality joins, especially if one table fits in memory.
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Cost at a glance
        
          Nested loop (unsorted)O(m × n)
          
        
        
          Sort-merge (pre-sorted)O(m + n)
          
        
        
          Hash (unsorted, equality)O(N + M)
          
        
        In theory both hash and merge perform well on large tables. The deciding factors are sortedness, the join predicate, memory, and whether a useful index exists.
      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Picking a Strategy
      Each strategy has a sweet spot
      In theory, both hash and merge joins perform well with large tables. A query engine typically uses a hash join for large, unsorted datasets and equality joins — especially if one table fits in memory.
      A merge join is often used when both tables are pre-sorted on the join key, or for range-based joins, since it minimizes memory usage and avoids the extra I/O of building a hash table.
      Nested loops, while the least scalable, may outperform both on small datasets or when a good index is available on the inner table, since they require the least overhead.
      
        Hash · big & unsorted
        Merge · pre-sorted / range
        Nested · small / indexed
      
    

    
      4.2 · Cost Comparison
      Sum beats product — when you can get it
      The headline difference is complexity. Nested loops can degrade to O(m × n) on unsorted inputs, while sort-merge and hash both scale with the sum of the inputs once their setup is paid for.
      But complexity isn't the whole story: merge pays a sort cost, hash pays to build a table and risks collisions, and nested loops carry almost no overhead. The optimizer weighs all of this against your actual data and indexes to choose the cheapest plan.
      
        Nested O(m × n)
        Merge O(m + n)
        Hash O(N + M)
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      Joins combine rows from multiple tables, most often by matching a key shared by both. Matching keys are usually identical in construction so they can correctly identify matches.
    
    
      02
      Logical join types define what the result holds: inner (A ∩ B), full outer (A ∪ B, m + n rows), left/right outer (at most m + n), and cross (A × B, m × n rows).
    
    
      03
      Logical vs. physical: whatever logical join you write, the optimizer chooses how to run it — nested loop, sort-merge, or hash — by building and comparing query plans.
    
    
      04
      Nested loop join scans the inner table for every outer row — O(m × n) on unsorted data. Indexing or sorting the inner table, or joining on a unique primary key, lets the search stop early.
    
    
      05
      Sort-merge join sorts both inputs, then sweeps them with twin pointers, advancing the smaller side. Each table is read at most once, so cost is proportional to m + n. Needs an equijoin predicate.
    
    
      06
      Hash join builds a hash table from the smaller input, then probes it with the larger one. Linear O(N + M), no sorting needed; watch for collisions, minimized by a good hash function.
    
    
      07
      Optimizer choice: hash for large unsorted equality joins, merge for pre-sorted or range joins, nested loops for small datasets or a well-indexed inner table.

title: SQL, Keys, Indexes, and B+ Trees
text:
● SQL Database Essentials
    SQL, Keys,Indexes &B+ Trees
    What is SQL? What are its primary features? How does it Index? How does a tree structures power billions of queries per second.
    
      SQL Foundations
      Keys & Constraints
      Index Structures
      B+ Tree Internals
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    What is SQL & how do tables work?
    SQL is the language of relational databases. Instead of storing everything in one spreadsheet or file, SQL stores related pieces of information in separate tables. Queries combine those tables only when needed.
  





  
  

    
    
      
        students table
        



student_id
name
major
year




1001
Alex
CS
Sophomore


1002
Maria
Math
Junior


1003
Sam
CS
Sophomore


1004
Nina
Physics
Senior




        
SELECT name, major
FROM students
WHERE year = 'Sophomore';
        
      
    

    
    
      
        
          
            students
            PKstudent_id
            name
            major
            year
          
          
            student_id
            ↔
            links
          
          
            enrollments
            PKenroll_id
            FKstudent_id
            course_id
            grade
          
        
        



enroll_id
student_id
course_id




E001
1001
CS301


E002
1001
CS401


E003
1003
CS301




      
    


    
      
        Defining keys in SQL
        
CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name        VARCHAR(100),
  major      VARCHAR(100)
);
CREATE TABLE enrollments (
  enrollment_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  student_id    INT,
  course_id    INT,
  FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
    REFERENCES students(student_id)
);
-- Add a primary key after creation:
ALTER TABLE students
ADD PRIMARY KEY (student_id);
-- Add a foreign key after creation:
ALTER TABLE enrollments
ADD CONSTRAINT fk_student
FOREIGN KEY (student_id)
REFERENCES students(student_id);
        
        
          
            PRIMARY KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE
          
          
            FOREIGN KEY
            Defined at table creation or added later with ADD CONSTRAINT
          
        
      
    

  

  
  

    
      1.1 · SQL Foundations
      SQL is declarative and remote
      SQL (Structured Query Language) lets you create, query, update, and manage relational databases. A relational database stores data in tables — rows are records, columns are attributes/variables.
      The key insight: SQL is declarative. You specify what data you want; the database engine's optimizer figures out how to retrieve it efficiently. This is why the same SQL query can run substantially faster with an index than without.
      SQL queries are sent to a server (SQL server) where the database is stored, though this server is often remote, that doesn't have to be the case. SQL handles everything in executing the query and returns the resulting data to the user.
      On the left is an example of a SQL query on the table students that retreives the name and major of Sophmore students in the table.
      
        Declarative
        Relational
        Structured
        Query Language
      
    

    
      1.2 · Tables & Relationships
      Relational aspect of SQL and Keys
      A table represents an entity — students, courses, orders. Each row is one record; each column is one attribute. A primary key uniquely identifies each row.
      A primary key must be unique and never NULL. Most tables have one primary key, though composite keys (multiple columns) are valid and identify rows through a composition of multiple columns.
      Relational databases become powerful when multiple tables are connected. To do this: A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key. A foreign key doesn't have to be made up of unique values but it does need to reference a primary key (which is made up of unique values). It enforces referential integrity — you cannot enroll a student who doesn't exist. The database prevents the inconsistency at the constraint level.
      The student_id column bridges the students and enrollments tables which enables powerful joins without redundancy. A foreign key defines and enforces a relationship between tables.
      
        Entities
        Attributes
        No Duplication
        Joins
      
    

    
      1.3 · Primary & Foreign Keys
      How to create keys in context
      On the left is the SQL command for creating primary and foreign keys in a SQL database.
      The CREATE TABLE command is used when one creates the key at the same time as creating the SQL table itself.
      The ALTER TABLE command is used when the table was already created but has no pre-defined key. For a primary key within the query one would use ADD PRIMARY KEY and for a foreign key within the query one would use ADD CONSTRAINT and must specify the constraint is a foreign key and the primary key that the foreign key references.
      Common confusion: a foreign key is simply a column in your table. The relationship is established from by declaring it as a reference to another table's primary key with a FOREIGN KEY constraint.
      
        Unique
        Not NULL
        Referential Integrity
        Composite Keys
      
    

  




  
    Key insight
    Keys define relationships. Indexes make those relationships fast to query.
    A primary key is a logical constraint (a column). An index is the underlying data structure the database mainatains to turn a key lookup from O(n) into O(log n). They coexist but are fundamentally different things.
  




  02
  
    Performance Structures
    Indexes
    An index is a separate ordered data structure that helps the database find rows faster by storing indexed column values and references to the corresponding table rows.
  





  

    
    
      
        Physical rows vs. Index order
        
          
            Physical table
            Row 1 Tom
            Row 2 Alex
            Row 4 Nina
            Row 7 Maria
            Row 9 Sam
          
          →
          
            Index on name
            Alex → Row 2
            Maria → Row 7
            Nina → Row 4
            Sam → Row 9
            Tom → Row 1
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_name
  ON students(name);
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_student
  ON enrollments(student_id);
        
      
    

    
    
      
        ❌ Full table scan (no index)
        
        Every row checked. Complexity: O(n).
        ✅ Index lookup
        
          Root
          →
          Branch
          →
          Leaf
          →
          Match!
        
        
          
            O(n)
            Full scan
          
          
            O(log n)
            Index lookup
          
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Index tradeoffs
        
          
            ✅ Reads
            Faster WHEREFaster JOINsEfficient ORDER BYFast range queries
          
          
            ⚠️ Writes
            Slower INSERTSlower UPDATESlower DELETEExtra storage
          
        
        
        Selectivity matters
        
          
            email = 'x@example.com'High ✓
            
            Matches ~1 row of millions
          
          
            status = 'active'Low ✗
            
            Matches 60% of rows — index barely helps
          
          
            country = 'US'Medium
            
            Depends on data distribution
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      2.1 · What is an Index
      An index is a sorted, separate access structure
      An index is a separate data structure — like a phonebook or a textbook's back index — that helps the database find rows without reading every single one.
      The physical rows in a table can be in any order. An index stores keys in sorted logical order with references (row pointers) to where those rows actually live. The table itself doesn't move.
      Important: a key is a logical constraint (unique, not null). An index is a physical performance structure. Primary keys usually get an index automatically in MySQL/InnoDB — but the concepts are distinct.
      
        Sorted Keys
        Row References
        Separate Structure
        Faster Lookup
      
    

    
      2.2 · Why Indexes Help
      Advantage: Prevents scanning every row, by following a guided path
      Without an index on email, finding WHERE email = 'x@example.com' requires inspecting every row — a full table scan. With millions of rows this leads to computing inefficiencies and prolonged runtimes.
      With an index, the database follows an ordered tree path — root → branch → leaf — touching only a handful of nodes to land on the match. Essentially, an index cuts down the total number of comparisons needed to identify a row. This is the difference between O(n), which is the runtime without an index, and O(log n) which is the decreased runtime through an index.
      Indexes are most impactful for WHERE filters, JOIN conditions, ORDER BY sorting, and range queries like BETWEEN. Below the structure and functionality of an index is explained in depth.
      
        WHERE clauses
        JOIN conditions
        ORDER BY
        Range queries
      
    

    
      2.3 · Limitations & Tradeoffs
      Indexes not always fast and beneficial
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes. Every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must also update every relevant index which causes the structure of the index to alter. Too many indexes increase maintenance cost and storage of the database.
      Selectivity is critical. An index on a column with few distinct values (like a boolean is_active or status) may not help — too many rows match, and the database may choose a full scan anyway since that may be more efficient.
       Although primary keys must be unique by definition, a regular index (created with CREATE INDEX) has no uniqueness requirement at all. Sometimes, regular indexes can be helpful to speed up row retrieval and querying even if they aren't for primary keys. It is still important to consider selectivity or uniqueness when creating a regular index, as an index on a column with low selectivity may not provide performance benefits and can even degrade performance due to the overhead of maintaining the index and the potential for increased table lookups.
        
      Even with a perfect index, a query can still be slow if it matches many rows (leaf scanning) or those rows are scattered across many storage pages and has to perform many table lookups. Table lookups happen when the database engine jumps from the index back to the main table to retrieve a full row of data after finding a match in the index. This is not a problem in MySQL because the table data lives directly in the primary key's B+ tree leaves (clustered index), so there's no second trip. Secondary indexes, however, (on non-primary-key columns) always require a second lookup back to the primary key tree to get the full row.
      
        Write overhead
        Low selectivity
        Leaf scanning
        Table lookups
      
    

  




  
    So what structure does an index use?
    Most database indexes are B trees. MySQL uses B+ trees which are a balanced tree structure designed for disk-based storage with high fanout and sorted leaf chains.
    Understanding the tree explains why indexes are fast for both equality lookups and range queries, and why they stay efficient even with millions of rows.
  




  03
  
    Data Structures
    B+ Trees
    The tree structure that makes indexes fast and optimized in MySQL.
  





  

    
    
      
        
          B+ Tree Structure
        
        
          
        
        
          Root node (top) → branch nodes (middle) → leaf nodes (bottom, linked in sorted order)
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Search path for key 57
        
          
            
              
                
              
              
                
              
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            
            

            
            39 | 83 | 98
            
            < 39
            46|53|57|83
            83-98
            
            42|48|51
            55 | 57 | 57
            61|72|79
            
            
            KEY 57 FOUND ✓
          
        
        
          1Root [39|83|98]: 57 > 39 and < 83 → middle pointer
          2Branch [46|53|57|83]: 57 = 57 → leaf pointer
          3Leaf [55|57|57]: key found, return row reference
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Why B+ trees stay shallow
        
          Fanout of 100 → 3 levels covers
          1,000,000
          leaf entries with only 3 page reads
        
        
        
          
            ROOT
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Br
            Br
            Br
            ...
          
          ↓ × many pointers
          
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            Lf
            ...
          
        
        
          
            Equality
            Root → leaf in O(log n)
          
          
            Range
            Walk sorted leaf chain
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · B+ Tree Structure
      Root, branches, leaves
      A B+ tree has three node types. The root at the top is the entry point for every search. Branch nodes in the middle store only keys and pointers — they guide the search but hold no row data. Leaf nodes at the bottom store the actual index entries and row references.
      In MySQL/InnoDB, the primary key index is clustered — the actual table row data lives in the leaf nodes of the primary key B+ tree. Secondary indexes store the indexed column value plus the corresponding primary key, requiring a second tree lookup to retrieve the full row.
      
        Root node
        Branch nodes
        Leaf nodes
        Clustered index
      
    

    
      3.2 · Leaf Chain
      Leaf nodes are linked for range scans
      One of the key advantages of B+ trees over regular binary trees: leaf nodes are linked left-to-right in sorted order. This dashed green chain in the diagram is what enables efficient range queries.
      To answer WHERE score BETWEEN 43 AND 67, the database traverses the tree to find 43, then simply walks the leaf chain to collect everything up to 67 — no re-traversal needed.
      
        Sorted chain
        Range scans
        BETWEEN
        ORDER BY
      
    

    
      3.3 · Tree Traversal
      Tree traversal walk-through
      Consider the task where the goal is to search for a key 57: start at the root [39|83|98]. Since 57 is between 39 and 83, follow the middle pointer. At branch [46|53|57|83], find the position of 57 and follow to the leaf [55|57|57]. Found.
      Notice that only one node is visited per level. This is always true for any key lookup in a B+ tree, regardless of the key or the size of the data. At each node, the search key is compared against the stored keys and exactly one pointer is followed downward. The result is that the number of nodes inspected is always exactly equal to the depth of the tree. This is where the O(log n) runtime comes from: each level cuts the search space by the branching factor, and the tree's depth grows only logarithmically with the number of rows.
      
        O(log n)
        One path
        Exact lookup
      
    

    
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Shallow trees handle millions of rows in 3–4 reads
      Each node in a B+ tree corresponds to one page (typically 16 KB in MySQL/InnoDB). Because a page can hold hundreds of keys, the fanout — the number of entries per node — is very high.
      With fanout 100: level 1 (root) covers 100 branches. Level 2 covers 10,000. Level 3 covers 1,000,000 leaves. Therefore, a tree covering a million rows reads only 3 pages for any lookup. That's why B+ trees scale so well.
      The tree is always balanced — all leaf nodes sit at the same depth — because insertions and deletions trigger automatic node splits and merges that propagate up the tree to maintain this property. This guarantees predictable search time regardless of which key you look up.
      
        Efficient
        Always balanced
        Predictable
      
    


 
      3.4 · Efficiency & Fanout
      Index structures differ across SQL dialects
      MySQL primarily uses B+ trees to structure its indexes, but other dialects treat indexing slightly differently. For example, PostgreSQL and SQLite default to B tree indexing, where (among other differences) row data is stored in all levels of the tree rather than just in the leaves. In Postgres, it is also possible to manually choose alternative indexing strategies like hash (simple and efficient for equality comparisons) or GiST operating classes (for geometric data and nearest-neighbor queries). Generally, though, understanding the structure of B and B+ trees is essential to creating and working with indexes in SQL.  
      
        B vs. B+ trees
        Manual index structure selection
      
    

  




  04
  
    Best Practices
    Practical indexing: What to take away from indexing
    Knowing the theory behind indexes can be helpful but only if you know how to utilize the information for your own good.
  





  

    
    
      
        Composite index: (customer_id, order_date)
        
          Sorted by customer_id first, then order_date within each customer:
          cust: 12024-01-05
          cust: 12024-03-12
          cust: 12024-07-01
          cust: 22024-02-18
          cust: 22024-09-30
          · · ·

          
            cust: 10
            2024-01-09 ← found
          
        
        
CREATE INDEX idx_cust_date
  ON orders(customer_id, order_date);
EXPLAIN
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE customer_id = 10
ORDER BY order_date;
        
      
    

    
    
      

        
        
          SQL indexes: OLTP vs OLAP
        

        
        

          
          
            
              ✅ OLTP
            
            Transactional
            
              Finds few specific rows
              B+ tree skips most data
              Joins on foreign keys
              WHERE, lookup, update
            
            O(log n) and index beneficial
          

          
          
            
              ⚠️ OLAP
            
            Analytical
            
              Scans many/all rows
              Index can't help a full scan
              Aggregates whole columns
              AVG, SUM, GROUP BY
            
            O(n) and index not beneficial
          

        

        
        
        
          
            Use SQL + B+ tree indexes for transactional row lookups.
            Use DuckDB / Snowflake for analytical column scans.
          
        

      
    

  

  

    
      4.1 · Composite Indexes
      Column order (during creation) in composite indexes is critical
      A composite index on (customer_id, order_date) sorts first by customer_id, then by order_date within each customer group. This makes queries that filter by customer_id alone very efficient.
      It also helps queries filtering by both columns, and sorting by order_date after filtering by customer_id. However, a query filtering only by order_date won't benefit much — the index isn't sorted by that column alone.
      What to take away: put the column you filter on most frequently first when creating a composite index. Doing so improves both lookup speed and query efficiency compared to any other column ordering.
      
        Leading column first
        Prefix matching
        Column order matters
      
    

    
      4.2 · Guidelines
      Indexes make SQL fast for row-based, transactional queries
      SQL indexes are purpose-built for OLTP — Online Transactional Processing. These are queries that locate and return a small number of specific rows: find one customer, update one order, look up one student. The B+ tree traverses from root to leaf in O(log n) and lands on exactly the rows you need, touching almost nothing else in the table.
      This is fundamentally different from analytical queries — the kind that ask "what is the average grade across all students?" or "how many orders were placed per month this year?" Those queries need to scan many or all rows and aggregate across entire columns. An index does not help here because there is no shortcut — every row must be read regardless. This workload is called OLAP (Online Analytical Processing).
      SQL finds the right rows fast through its indexes, but analytical queries need to read one column across millions of rows meaning the database still has to touch every row's storage page. This makes analytical queries less efficient in SQL compared to transactional queries (row-based). However, based on the index model of SQL, SQL is especially efficient and strong for row-based queries.
      The practical takeaway: use SQL and its B+ tree indexes for transactional workloads like precise lookups, joins on foreign keys, filtering by specific values. For large-scale analytical work across many rows and few columns, a column-oriented system is the better architectural choice (like DuckDB or Snowflake).
      
        OLTP
        Row lookup
        OLAP
        Column scan
      
    

  




  Key Takeaways
  
    
      01
      SQL is a declarative language, a query is sent to a remote SQL server, and the optimizer decides how to retrieve it efficiently. Rows are records, columns are attributes.
    
    
      02
      Primary keys uniquely identify rows and must be unique and never NULL. Foreign keys are columns that reference another table's primary key. They create relationships and enforce referential integrity. Both can be defined at table creation or added later with ALTER TABLE.
    
    
      03
      Indexes are separate physical structures, distinct from keys, that store column values in sorted order with row references. They reduce lookups from O(n) full scans to O(log n) guided tree traversal. Composite indexes require putting the most-filtered column first for maximum efficiency.
    
    
      04
      B+ trees power MySQL indexes. The root is the entry point, branch nodes guide the search, and leaf nodes store the data. Leaf nodes are linked in sorted order enabling fast range scans. With high fanout, just 3–4 levels can cover millions of rows.
    
    
      05
      Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes — every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE must also update the index structure. Low-selectivity columns (like booleans or status fields) benefit little from indexing since too many rows match.
    
    
      06
      SQL indexes excel at OLTP, precise row lookups, joins on foreign keys, transactional queries. For OLAP analytical workloads that scan entire columns across millions of rows, column-oriented systems like DuckDB or Snowflake are the better architectural choice.

title: File Formats, Parquet & DuckDB
text:
● Data Storage Essentials
    File Formats,Parquet &DuckDB
    How data is stored on disk shapes how fast you can query it along with the space it takes up on the disk. From row-based CSVs to columnar Parquet files to embedded analytical databases every format has benefits and trade-offs.
    
      Storage Layouts
      Parquet Internals
      DuckDB Architecture
      When to Use What
    
    Scroll to explore↓
  




  01
  
    Foundations
    File formats & storage layouts
    Before writing a single query, the data has to live somewhere. How a file arranges its bytes on disk — row by row, column by column, or a hybrid of both — determines what kinds of queries run fast and what kinds run slow.
  





  

    
    
      
        Storage layout comparison
        

          
          
            Original Table
            
              
                Col A
                Col B
                Col C
                Col D
              
              
                A1B1C1D1
              
              
                A2B2C2D2
              
              
                A3B3C3D3
              
            
            3 rows × 4 columns. Is the logical table before any storage layout is applied.
          

          
          
             Row-Based (CSV, JSON)
            
              A1B1C1D1
              A2B2C2D2
              A3B3C3D3
            
            All columns per row stored together on disk
          

          
          
            Columnar (Pure Columnar)
            
              A1A2A3A4
              B1B2B3B4
              C1C2C3C4
              D1D2D3D4
            
            Each column stored contiguously — read only what you need
          

          
          
            Hybrid (Parquet) — Row Groups
            
              Row Group 1
              
                A1A2
                B1B2
                C1C2
                D1D2
              
              Row Group 2
              
                A3A4
                B3B4
                C3C4
                D3D4
              
            
            Rows chunked into groups; within each group, stored columnarly
          

        
      
    

    
    
      
        Format comparison
        



Feature
CSV
JSON
Parquet




Layout
Row
Row
Hybrid


Human-readable
Yes
Yes
No


Built-in compression
No
No
Yes


Schema embedded
No
Partial
Yes


Query efficiency
Low
Low
High


Typical file size
Medium
Large
Small


Best for
Simple exports
APIs / config
Analytics




      
    

    
    
      
        Relative file size — same dataset
        
          
            JSON
            
              1,834,260 bytes
            
          
          
            CSV
            
              829,667 bytes
            
          
          
            Parquet
            
              
  327,738 bytes

            
          
        
        
          Why is Parquet so much smaller?
          
            Columnar storage groups similar values together, making compression extremely effective. Parquet also uses dictionary encoding, replacing repeated values like "California" with a tiny integer code across all occurrences.
          
        
        
          
            Parquet
            1×
            baseline
          
          
            CSV
            2.5×
            larger
          
          
            JSON
            5.6×
            larger
          
        
      
    

  

  

    
      1.1 · Storage Layouts
      How a file stores its bytes changes everything
      A main distinction between file storage tupes is how the data is grouped, mainly row by row or column by column. This different impacts what kinds of queries and tasks perform more efficiently.
      A row-based format like CSV or JSON stores all of a record's fields together. Reading one row is fast, but reading a single column across millions of rows means touching every row's data on disk, even the columns you don't care about.
      A columnar format stores all values from one column together. Analytical queries that compute aggregates over one or two columns out of dozens skip the irrelevant columns entirely, the disk reads are far smaller and thus more efficient.
      Parquet uses a hybrid: it partitions rows into large row groups (typically 100K–1M rows each) for large datasets, and within each group stores data columnarly. This gives columnar compression benefits while keeping related rows together for efficient scanning.
      
        Row-based
        Columnar
        Hybrid
        Row Groups
      
    

    
      1.2 · Formats Compared
      CSV, JSON, and Parquet serve different purposes
      CSV is the simplest and typically most standard file type. It is a plain-text file where every value is separated by a comma representing each row at a time. Anyone can open it in a spreadsheet and read it. But it has no schema (a formal record of column names and their data types), no type information, no compression, and the database has to infer everything.
      JSON is more expressive, it supports nested objects and arrays, making it ideal for APIs and configuration. But nesting means the structure is irregular, which makes bulk analysis slow and produces large files. JSON is the least efficient storage format for analytics and typically the largest in size.
      Parquet is purpose-built for analytical workloads and storing information efficiently (compactly). It is not human-readable, in binary format but it stores the full schema in its footer, supports built-in column-level compression, and enables query engines to skip entire row groups that can't match a filter using stored statistics (located in the footer of the file).
      
        CSV: simple & portable
        JSON: flexible & verbose
        Parquet: fast & compact
      
    

    
      1.3 · Compression & Encoding
      Columnar storage compresses information more efficiently
      The graphic on the left comes from a real experiment comparing the same dataset stored in all three formats. Each format stores the exact same information from the Stanford Open Policing Project dataset from Los Angeles. However, the difference is striking in file size: the Parquet file is over 5× smaller than JSON and nearly 2.5× smaller than CSV.
      This happens for two reasons. First, columnar grouping means similar values sit next to each other in the file,  compression algorithms like Snappy or Zstd exploit this repetition extremely well. Second, Parquet uses dictionary encoding: if a string column contains the same 50 city names repeated millions of times, Parquet replaces each string with a compact integer and stores a single dictionary. The savings compound to make a strong disparity.
      Smaller files mean fewer bytes read from disk, which is usually the primary bottleneck in data analysis. Parquet's compression is not just a storage benefit, it is a query performance benefit too.
      
        Dictionary encoding
        Snappy / Zstd compression
        Less disk I/O
        Schema in footer
      
    

  




  
    Fun historical insight
    Parquet was actually made by Twitter, and Google aided in its creation.
    Parquet's origin story actually traces back to a 2010 Google research paper called "Dremel: Interactive Analysis of Web-Scale Datasets", which introduced the idea of representing nested data in a columnar format. This trick let Google query massive datasets interactively instead of waiting on slow batch jobs.Three years later, in 2013, two unlikely co-conspirators Twitter and Cloudera joined forces and built Parquet together and donated it to the Apache Software Foundation, making it free and open for anyone to use. Twitter needed to crunch enormous volumes of data fast, and Cloudera was deep in the business of helping companies do exactly that at scale. Today Parquet is widely known as a open source file storage format purpose-built for analytical OLAP-style workloads, heavy on reads and aggregations, over huge piles of data, and it's remarkably space-efficient.

      2.1 · File Structure
      A Parquet file has more than just data
      Every Parquet file starts and ends with the four magic bytes PAR1. This simply identify's the file on the computer as a Parquet file. Between the magic bytes, the data is divided into row groups: horizontal partitions, each containing a chunk of rows. Within each row group, every column's data is stored as its own contiguous column chunk, further split into pages. Pages are the smallest unit of storage in Parquet. Each page holds a batch of values for one column, and a column chunk is simply a sequence of these pages read one after another.
      The footer at the end of the file is critical and unique to parquet files compared to JSON or CSV. It holds the full schema (column names and data types), the byte offset (the exact position in the file, measured in bytes from the start, where each row group begins) of every row group, and per-column statistics including minimum and maximum values. A query engine reads the footer first and uses it to build a plan before reading any actual data. 
      The footer is created automatically when the Parquet file is written. The software writing the file (such as DuckDB, Spark, or the R arrow package) tracks the schema, byte offsets, and min/max statistics as it writes each row group, then writes the metadata into the footer as the very last step before closing the file.
      This design means a tool like DuckDB can open a Parquet file, read the footer in milliseconds, and immediately know which row groups can be skipped for a given query filter,without touching any data pages.
      
        PAR1 magic bytes
        Row groups
        Column chunks
        Footer metadata
      
    

    
      2.2 · Predicate Pushdown
      Row group skipping and why it is beneficial
      Parquet's footer stores the minimum and maximum value of every column in every row group, which applies to any data type. This enables a technique called predicate pushdown: pushing a filter condition down into the storage layer so data that can't possibly match is never loaded.
      Consider WHERE date > '2023-06-01' against a file with five row groups. The footer reveals row groups 1–3 have max dates before the cutoff. The engine skips them entirely. Only groups 4 and 5 are read. If you have 100 row groups and the filter eliminates 90, you read 10% of the file.
      This is a form of metadata-driven pruning. It doesn't require a B+ tree index since the statistics in the footer are sufficient. It is one of the main reasons Parquet files can be queried efficiently by tools that don't load the data into a database first.
      
        Predicate pushdown
        Min/max statistics
        Row group pruning
        No index needed
      
    

    
      2.3 · When to Use Parquet
      Parquet Benefits
      Use Parquet when your workflow is analytical. In other words, when your frequent queries aggregate, filter, or summarize across many rows. It outperforms CSV and JSON on these workloads not by a small margin but by an order of magnitude, because it reads far fewer bytes from disk.
      Parquet's universality is also a major advantage. It works seamlessly across the entire modern data stack — Spark, DuckDB, Pandas, Polars, BigQuery, Snowflake, Athena, and Hive all read and write Parquet natively, as do R packages like arrow and dplyr via dbplyr. Choosing Parquet as your storage format means you can utilize many various tools.
      The only reason to prefer CSV or JSON over Parquet is human readability and editability. If someone needs to open the file in a text editor or spreadsheet, CSV would be better. If you're building an API response payload, JSON would be better. If you're storing data for analysis, Parquet is almost always the better choice.
      
        Analytical workloads
        Universal compatibility
        Schema evolution
        Not human-readable

    DuckDB can query Parquet files directly on disk with no load step. It combines Parquet's columnar storage efficiency with full SQL expressiveness and the simplicity of an embedded database.
